I did some more research on this and it looks like most terminals don't distinguish between resetters for bold and faint. They use a single sequence to reset both (bold and faint) at the same time. 

This means I have to merge `rs.bold` and `rs.faint` into something like `rs.bold_faint` and `rs.faint_bold`. The result would look a little bit ugly, e.g.: `ef.bold + "foo" + rs.bold_faint`, but I guess it's the only way.

I just ran into something similar today, also on Win7 x64, python 3.5. 

I was getting an exception on line 281 when reading a gzip compressed NRRD:

```decompressed_data += decompobj.decompress(chunk)```

I traced the problem back to the use of mmap on line 243 where mmap is instantiated. Following that call if you check the file position with 'datafilehandle.tell()' you find it returns -8192. The subsequent call to datafilehandle.seek() does not fix it. When the header is read it advances the header size number of bytes but is still probably negative. One quick fix is
```
m = mmap.mmap(datafilehandle.fileno(), 0, access=mmap.ACCESS_READ)
datafilehandle.tell()
datafilehandle.seek(0)
```
Calling `tell()` seems to get things unstuck so the following `seek` works. Just calling `seek`, even doing it multiple times, does not work. A subsequent `tell()` **still** returns -8192. The next  `seek` is successful.

One workaround is to call `tell` and `seek` as above although I hate to rely on magical incantations like that. I think a better choice would be to get rid of mmap as it is only used to find the end of the header. Assuming that the header is a reasonable size the following can replace the mmap code starting on line 241:
```
    datafilehandle.seek(0)  # Win is open in binary and others in normal mode
    chunk = datafilehandle.read(_READ_CHUNKSIZE)
    seek_past_header_pos = chunk.find(b'\n\n')
```
I only tried that on Win7-64 & python 3 so it might need adjustment for backwards compatibility.

* it would be nice to maintain some kind of backwards compatibility as a general rule, especially in the yaml format

Here is a workaround I use for this issue:
in `config_dev.py` file, after `DEBUG = True` I added the following code:
```
DEBUG = True
# avoid frustrating INFO messages about timeout caused by sleep(1)
import logging
class SkipTimeouts(logging.Filter):
    def filter(self, rec):
        if(rec.levelno == logging.INFO and
           rec.msg.startswith('poll') and
           rec.msg.endswith(': timeout') and
           990 < rec.args[0] < 1000 and
           1000 < rec.args[1] < 1010):
            return False  # hide this record
        return True
logging.getLogger('asyncio').addFilter(SkipTimeouts())
```

You're using Celery 3.0.x with a flower version requiring Celery 3.1!

I'm just a user, so don't take this as anything official, but that's not really the kind of monitoring Flower does.

Because of all the different brokers that Celery can use, and the usage patterns that can exist for any environment, it would be hard to reliably get useful information for this based on how Flower is querying the Celery workers.  There are dozens of off-the-shelf tools for monitoring your broker directly and generate alerts based on that -- Nagios, Munin, etc for running your own service and subscription cloud-based services like Datadog and New Relic.

These all do a much better job than trying to get Flower to reinvent the wheel and add a huge feature outside of its scope.

Thought about it.   I'm going to go one better than adding a noisy warning about this.  I added an outright failure with a `RuntimeError` if anyone uses an async generator outside of a guarding context manager.   So, you have to do this:

     from curio import async_generator

     async def coro():
         async with async_generator(some_generator()) as agen:
                async for item in agen:
                      ...


I'm not totally sure on the name.  Nathaniel used `aclose()` for the context manager in earlier writing.  I have used the name `async_generator()` here solely because it more explicitly states what it's being used for.   I'm not married to that though.  Open to suggestions on naming.

Admittedly, I don't consider it ideal to have to use the enclosing context manager.  However, in the big picture, I'd rather have correctness than a bunch of tricky acrobatics going on in the kernel to make async generator finalization work "correctly."

So uh... I feel like I should point out that the way actual shells do this doesn't require creating two pipe objects and copying from one to the other. Instead they just make one pipe object and let the two processes talk to each other directly without the parent having to do anything at all.

```python
groff = Popen(groffcmd, stdin=PIPE, stdout=PIPE)
zcat = Popen(zcatcmd, stdout=groff.stdin)
```

In a different thread I have suggested to label units not only by their dimensions but also their role. (I do not have a better name). It's purpose is to provide a way to distinguish between quantities with the same same units but different "extended dimensionality". (See #505)

I think something like this could help also in your case if we allow roles to be provided on the fly
```
>>> q1 = ureg.Quantity(1.0, 'kg:salt^3/kg:water^3')
>>> q1
<Quantity(1.0, 'm^3/m^3')> 
>>> q2 = ureg.Quantity(2.0, 'kg:salt^3/kg:water^3')
>>> q2
<Quantity(1.0, 'm^3/m^3')> 
>>> q1 / q2
<Quantity(0.5, dimensionless)> 
```

Nobody has opened yet a discussion about the API and how it should be implemented, but I think it is something worth pursuing.

The reason why we convert to `utc` in `datetime_to_timestamp` is that `calendar.timegm` assumes the input to be in UTC:

https://docs.python.org/2/library/calendar.html#calendar.timegm
https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html#time.gmtime

The reason we use `calendar.timegm` and not `time.mktime` is that `time.mktime()` might use different epoch, depending on the platform:

> The epoch is the point where the time starts, and is platform dependent. For Unix, the epoch is January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 (UTC). To find out what the epoch is on a given platform, look at time.gmtime(0).

https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html

While for `calendar`, it is hardcoded to the POSIX Epoch, 1970-01-01UTC.

Just a note that this will be fixed in #102.

For everyone who's following this, Atlasssian is saying that XMPP will *not* be supported, leaving Will and all other current integrations out in the cold.   They say that this is a technical decision, and thus far, have not been open to feedback from partners that XMPP is a crucial bit of the stack.

At this point, here's what I'd recommend if your company depends on a Will-Hipchat integration:

1. Please contact Atlasssian via the [contact the CEOs](https://www.atlassian.com/company/contact/contact-ceos) form, and tell them that your company relies on XMPP functionality, and that it's critical that Stride support it.

2. Upgrade to Will 2, and start investigating other options.  Slack is the obvious one, but Will also supports rocket.chat, and is very open to adding new backends.

3. If switching off HipChat isn't viable, sign up for the Stride beta, keep a close eye on the specs, and consistently provide feedback that you'll need a real-time, non-public endpoint way for bots to interact.  I've personally moved off of HipChat at my orgs, so I'm not going to be as sharp on this as someone with a vested interest.

**To be absolutely clear**: If Atlasssian provides a way for us to get Will (and bots, generally) to interact with Stride, we'll build it in to Will.  But we're completely at their mercy, and they will be killing off the HipChat XMPP functionality that Will relies on at some point.  If your org depends on it, I'd start making other plans.

If anyone gets more information, please add it to this issue - hoping this all gets resolved well, and soon!

The audio isn't perfectly smooth. The fundamental problem is the threading model in python.

I think the threading model in python isn't performant enough to keep up with audio because of the global interpreter lock. One approach to fix it would be to run the audio stream and sending messages to the controller in a separate process using the multiprocessing module and have the main process pass messages about volume, led, rumble to that process via queues.

OMG, thanks for quick response! I missed a notification and only now remembered that I've asked it (blush).

> It's possible to parse Python with recursive descent

Great, thanks for reassuring me on that, maybe then one day I'll try to implement it...

>  but I recommend that you take a better approach, and parse it with LALR(1). It's a simple state-machine + stack

Right, and MicroPython already implements one of LR parsers using explicit stack automaton: https://github.com/micropython/micropython/blob/master/py/parse.c
 
The problem is that managing explicit stack still leads to overhead, the biggest of which being memory fragmentation overhead. Just consider that MicroPython is designed for systems with e.g. 16KB of total memory. Say, 4K of that is reserved for C stack, and mere 12KB for heap. If we start to allocate parser stack on the heap, heap becomes fragmented, and user app can't allocate large enough objects. Instead, all parsing state could be kept on C stack, and that's exactly what recursive decent parser does.

Summing up, there appears to be 3 choices:

1. LR parser + AST - current MicroPython's approach.
2. Keep LR (LALR) parser, but avoid AST and generate bytecode directly.
3. Use LL/recursive decent parser and generate bytecode directly.

3rd approach seems to be the most frugal, and that's my idea - if going for implementing an alternative parser, go for a solution which promises the most savings. Maybe it needs more consideration though.

Thanks again for discussion!

RFC 7519 says:
> The "iat" (issued at) claim identifies the time at which the JWT was issued.

In all circumstances except clock skew, it doesn't seem like it makes any sense for a JWT to be valid if, by its own claim, it hasn't been created yet. That's why we included this validation. JWT doesn't say you have to have this validation to be compliant, but it seems logical and enforces integrity in the data.

I'm against removing this validation, especially when you can already easily disable it by using ```jwt.decode(token, secret, options={'verify_iat': False})``` or account for the clock skew by using the leeway parameter: ```jwt.decode(token, secret, leeway=10)```

We've discussed that issue in the Django project, and didn't opt for the `format` syntax in all cases. The legacy placeholder syntax is not deprecated and is fine for many use cases.

I decided to go ahead and fix this.

Pydash can now support that `key[index]` format:

```
>>> import pydash
>>> o = [{"hits": {"hits": [{"_source": 5}]}}]
>>> pydash.deep_get(o, '[0].hits.hits[0]._source')
5
>>> pydash.deep_get(o, '[0].hits.hits.[0]._source')
5
```

Also as a consequence, things like this work too:

```
>>> o = [{"hits": {"hits": [{"_source": 5}]}}]
>>> pydash.deep_get(o, '[0]hits.hits[0]_source')
5
>>> o = [{'hits': [{'hits': [{'_source': 5}]}]}]
>>> pydash.deep_get(o, '[0]hits[0]hits[0]_source')
5
```

I've released this fix as v3.4.3: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pydash/3.4.3

Closing the issue for now, but feel free to re-open if you find any other issues related to this.

in my view, this is actually more of a problem with the yaml encoder than anything else. The problem is basically that in python 3, there is technically no way to directly represent a string in a serialized way. Python 3 strings can use several different in memory representations (see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0393/)

So basically @gward s change is an improvement because it makes explicit how the unicode string is serialized. The problem is that in python 3, the string is then encoded as a byte string which has this (non human readable representation) when it is serialized as yaml.

I may not be groking all of this correctly, and it may be that something else is going on, but i think that we would have to fix this in the serializer, rather than by changing what @gward did which was the right thing.

[python-modernize](https://github.com/python-modernize/python-modernize) is another project which may be able to benefit from this. It needs roundtripping and the ability to parse code from other Python versions. It's currently built on top of lib2to3, but there are various annoyances that are harder than they should be to fix because the code responsible is in 2to3.

However, as some other people has mentioned, I'm not sure that we have the time/energy to move it to a new parser (I'm not volunteering; cc @daira). The Dynamic Importer (implementation: https://git.gnome.org/browse/pygobject/tree/gi/importer.py#n126 ) loads GObject-Intropsection enabled libraries. It implements the PEP302 Importer Protocol.

It uses the IntrospectionModule class from https://git.gnome.org/browse/pygobject/tree/gi/module.py#n111 which is a wrapper around the introspection typelib. The actual wrapping is done by ``__getattr__()``. This class implements a ``__dir__()`` method which basically shows how to get a list of properties / methods of GObjects in pygobject.
 
The actual loading is done in C, by this file https://git.gnome.org/browse/pygobject/tree/gi/pygi-repository.c (methods accessible by Python are defined in line 304). It's done by using the GObject Introspection library to load info about the libraries from the .typelib files which describe all the interfaces, properties, functions and such that the libraries provide.

Here's some example code to get a list of properties of a GObject-Introspection library (module):
```python
from gi import Repository
r = Repository.get_default()
r.require('Gtk') # See note about versions
print(r.get_infos('Gtk'))
```

For each object in this result set you can use .get_fields(), .get_properties() and similar methods to get their "children" and .get_name() to get the actual object name that can be used for completion.

Note about versions:
by default the .require() method (and the actual import in user code) selects the newest version available of the library. However, different major versions might be installed and might have slightly different APIs, so PyGObject has gi.require_version() and it asks users to do it before the import so they get the major version they want.
The second parameter for .require() in your code would be the version string if gi.require_version was called. This is an example call:
gi.require_version('Ide', '1.0')

The first parameter is the "namespace" - the name of the library/module you want to import, and the second parameter is the version.

Is this clear enough? I could probably do more research and dive further into specifics.

FWIW I don't think there's a problem with the #include that is wrapped with the ifdef (https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/e481df9568640fcd5ddb98742ad37b9f26e56177/psutil/_psutil_windows.c#L21) since this can't cause linking errors at runtime. Only functions that will be in the import section of the produced DLL can cause the "DLL load failed error", e.g. the use of "GetIfEntry2" (https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/e481df9568640fcd5ddb98742ad37b9f26e56177/psutil/_psutil_windows.c#L2227) - which can be bypassed the same way as GetTickCount64.

However, even if the DLL will load correctly after fixing these calls, there could still be invalid memory access (=crashes) on older systems due to incorrect struct sizes in other places where we use extended classes (e.g. https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/e481df9568640fcd5ddb98742ad37b9f26e56177/psutil/_psutil_windows.c#L741). These places can also be fixed by determining the Window version at runtime, if we want to do all this work.

In any case, the #include is only for compile time so it will not be a problem as long as we avoid using its definitions on XP. Just my two cents.

Agree with @dpkp that the JMX interface + Zookeeper is first place I'd be setting alerts. The places you'd use Kafka-python are for monitoring kafka consumer lag, or overall cluster throughput validation/latency monitoring (a script that sends a message every so often, then tries to consume it to verify the message showed up at a reasonable time).

For just overall "liveliness", a very primitive way to monitor is to watch the ephemeral znode created by the brokers in Zookeeper to verify it's showing up. But really, I would suggest not rolling your own check scripts for Kafka by hand... there are a ton of them out there for most every monitoring system from nagios to graphite to ELK to vendors like datadog and signalfx.

Hi Stephen,
Okay, the error there is coming up because the mass range specified is mostly (I think  >80%) outside the range of the fit that you're using (I think the default fit is Tinker08, but you can check which it's using by `hmf.mf_fit`). 

In general, there's two ways of getting rid of this warning -- either change the mass range, or when instantiating, use  `hmf = MassFunction(cut_fit=False)`. However, the latter should give you exactly the same result that you have now (just without the warning).

The odd thing is, this warning shouldn't be coming up for the default example. The default mass range is 10^10 - 10^15 h^-1 M_sun, which is precisely the Tinker08 mass range. So you can check those values (check `hmf.Mmin` and `hmf.Mmax`). 

Cheers,
Steven

Hi @ciadch! What you are seeing is called the [flash of unstyled content](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flash_of_unstyled_content), FOUC for short. Although, it's not due to the stylesheet being loaded after the DOM is ready - it is due to the table not having any style applied initially. In order to prevent this from happening, add the class names that will be applied to the table element after it becomes initialized by tablesorter: ```html ...
``` * `table-bootstrap` is the class name used by tablesorter for the Bootstrap theme. * `table table-bordered table-striped` are class names used by Bootstrap. To get a better idea of this issue, check out [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/njj5b5vc/33/) where I use a `setTimeout` to delay tablesorter initialization.

Hi @fabio-C, first of all sorry for the delay in replying I've been away from my office for several weeks now. Translate is currently not supported, but I can add it in the next release. The trouble is that you need to fill the empty space with some background colour (or transparency) and there are several ways to do that (such as extrapolating) so I will have to work on that before I can add it. 

The code kindly provided by @ivicts will work but only if you are passing images **one at at time** through the pipeline (i.e. **without any masks**). This code here needs to be modified to be outside of the `do()` function so that the randomisation occurs once upon being called and the same values are used for each image passed through `do()`:

```python
random_left = random.randint(self.max_left_translation, 0)
random_right = random.randint(0, self.max_right_translation)
left_or_right = random.randint(0, 1)
translation_left_right = 0
if left_or_right == 0:
    translation_left_right= random_left
elif left_or_right == 1:
    translation_left_right = random_right

random_down = random.randint(self.max_down_translation, 0)
random_up = random.randint(0, self.max_up_translation)
up_or_down = random.randint(0, 1)
translation_up_down = 0
if up_or_down == 0:
    translation_up_down = random_up
elif up_or_down == 1:
    translation_up_down = random_down
```
So all the above code needs to be outside of `do()` and be executed once upon being called by the pipeline.

So @fabio-C, if you are willing to wait a week or so I can get this functionality added to the next version of Augmentor. Meanwhile you will need to rely on using a custom class, like that provided by @ivicts, but with the edits I have provided here (unless you are not using masks, in which case it will work fine).

I did not know that the para-virtualized network drivers return `-1` to `/sys/class/net/<iface>/speed`.

`ethtool` seems to accept `SPEED_UNKNOWN` (`-1`):
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/a090bd4ff8387c409732a8e059fbf264ea0bdd56/include/uapi/linux/ethtool.h#L1509

```c
static inline int ethtool_validate_speed(__u32 speed)
{
	return speed <= INT_MAX || speed == SPEED_UNKNOWN;
}
```

https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/a090bd4ff8387c409732a8e059fbf264ea0bdd56/include/uapi/linux/ethtool.h#L1652

```
 * struct ethtool_link_settings - link control and status
...
 * is up they represent the negotiated link mode; if the link is down,
 * the speed is 0, %SPEED_UNKNOWN or the highest enabled speed and
 * @duplex is %DUPLEX_UNKNOWN or the best enabled duplex mode.
```

>So would it make sense to add a handling in the code to default to the iproute2 upper bound when it encounters a -1 speed?

Sorry for this confusion, but ``grpclib`` supports only ``Python >= 3.5`` (intentionally) and only ``asyncio`` (currently), documented this in the README as much as I can. You can use ``grpcio`` library from Google if you have to develop with Python 2.7 support.

We are in our company using ``grpcio`` from the client-side (legacy project on Python 2.7), and going to use ``grpclib`` on the server-side (new services on Python >= 3.5).

HappyBase just calls the Thrift API, propagating any flags like `wal` to the Thrift API, so it's up to the Thrift API, and outside Happybases' control to do something sensible with that flag. Happybase doesn't do any "smart things" under the hood.

Just a point of clarification: The common AutoML use case doesn't involve packaging the AutoML tool as a part of some product, which is the only case that the LGPL license will matter. Nearly all use cases I've seen in the wild involve using AutoML to find a pipeline for a problem, and exporting that pipeline to use independently from the AutoML tool. That use case is not affected by the LGPL.

The downside of the more permissive licenses (from a developer perspective) is that it makes it easy to take an open source AutoML tool and build a "AutoMLaaS" company around it, effectively cashing in on the developers' hard work without giving anything back to them or the open source community. This has already happened to the auto-sklearn developers, and they were not happy about it.

Looks like the structure of dirs/files might have something to do with the issue. In cefpython on Mac we didn't use the official structuring from CEF. In cefclient the subprocess executable is named "cefclient Helper" and there are three kinds of it in the "cefclient.app/Contents/Frameworks" directory:

* cefclient Helper.app
* cefclient Helper EH.app
* cefclient Helper NP.app

This is the same executable, but duplicated in two different directories with the "EH" and "NP" postsfixes - I'm not sure what these are supposed to mean. In cefpython there is only one helper - the subproces executable. But the error and its path "/anaconda/lib/subprocess EH.app" suggests that the subprocess expects the .app folder structure. It expects that the helper app has such structure:

> cefclient Helper EH.app/Contents/MacOS

But instead the helper is here:

> /anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cefpython3/subprocess

So it does "../../../" to go out of the  "cefclient Helper EH.app/", but as we don't follow the official app structure, it ends up doing:

> /anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cefpython3/../../../ cefclient Helper EH.app/...

which becomes:

> /anaconda/lib/cefclient Helper EH.app/...

To resolve the issue it needs to be investigated why it looks for these helpers: ".. EH.app" and ".. NP.app". During my testing on Mac I've never had this issue, it always used only the default subprocess executable. What do the EH and NP names mean? It has some meaning for sure. Maybe this could be fixed by providing some flag/setting, so that it uses the default helper app.

In this case we have told the broker what the session end time is and to consider any bar with that given time (and not only those wiith a time greater than) to be the end of the session.

As such an order placed at 09:05 is executed at 17:30 and notified with the 17.30 bar.

Here the execution is the same but `eosbar` has not been indicated and as such the notification occurs when the system first sees a bar which is above the end of the session. Hence the notification on the next day at 09:05 rather than on the same day exactly at 17:30

`eosbar` has been added to the broker to support that.

```python
class BrokerBack(with_metaclass(MetaParams, object)):
    '''Broker Simulator
      Parameters:

        Note: use the setXXXX to set the value after instance creation

        - ``cash`` (default: 10000): starting cash

        - ``commission`` (default: CommInfoBase(percabs=True))
          base commission scheme which applies to all assets

        - ``checksubmit`` (default: True)
          check margin/cash before accepting an order into the system

        - ``eosbar`` (default: False):
          With intraday bars consider a bar with the same ``time`` as the end
          of session to be the end of the session. This is not usually the
          case, because some bars (final auction) are produced by many
          exchanges for many products for a couple of minutes after the end of
          the session
    '''
    params = (
        ('cash', 10000.0),
        ('commission', CommInfoBase(percabs=True)),
        ('checksubmit', True),
        ('eosbar', False),
    )
```

This was an original part of the plan and some of the initial internal api/methods can actually copy with more than one broker. Having been discarded to avoid extra complexities and having looked at the actual implementation of some methods (especially what refers to *notifications*), the introduction of multiple brokers for a single strategy would break many existing methods.

A rewrite/consolidation of some other internals would be of greater use for further extensions of the framework and later the introduction of such feature.

Given the above, it will not be happening any time soon in this series of versions

I think here two categories of users clashes with different requirements. 

First group is regular users which installs package via direct `pip install` as third party dependency and would like to use it right now. They don't interested in internal tests as they will never run them (and it's a shame that installation process doesn't involves any checks if package is not broken, but that's another sad story) and they also doesn't interested in builtin docs as they prefer read them on some service like RTD or PythonHosted where they are pretty rendered. So it's indeed sounds unreasonable to boat tarballs with the bits that will never be used.

Second group is package maintainers who want to install things right: check tarball signature (if any), run tests before make a package, pregenerate docs and put them into specific directory etc. These people needs all the bits all the time to provide high quality of their work and service for their users. 

So two groups needs different tarballs, but unlikely you would like to maintain two versions of releases. Responsibility reduction is a good thing and that's why to provide one release tarball that suites everyone.

I have been using this myself and I encounter the following situation:
I need to restrict my generated data to some constraint that either
   ```  a)``` is specific enough that using `filter` would be unusably slow
   `  b)` involves sharing data with another piece of generated data.
The way I have fixed this is by passing in a dictionary of `{'field' : strategy}` along with the data type. 
This allows for addressing `a)`. One example would be a `PositivePoint` type (but you will have this problem regardless of whether or not you are enforcing a constraint in the constructor)
```python
Point = NamedTuple('Point', [('x', int), ('y', int)])
# do some subclassing magic to create a `PositivePoint` has a constructor that raises errors on negative input
# the only way to avoid exceptions is something like the following
positive_point_strat = type_to_strat(Point, {'x' : st.integer(min_value=0), 'y' : st.integer(min_value=0)})
```
The problem with this is that the names can clash. NB: `func_to_start` uses the arg names in the dict. So use qualified names for (nested) NamedTuple fields:
```python
def f(point: PositivePoint, y: int): pass

f_strat = func_to_strat(f, {'point.x' : st.integer(min_value=0), 'point.y' : st.integer(min_value=0)})
```

and `b)` can sometimes be solved by using `st.shared` with a shared key.

Here's an updated example that might be more clear, it shows that in the `outer()` method, `self` is a regular `C` object rather than a `CProxy` object:

```
import wrapt

class C(object):
    def outer(self):
        print(type(self))
        return self.inner()

    def inner(self):
        return 7

class CProxy(wrapt.ObjectProxy):
    def inner(self):
        return self.__wrapped__.inner() + 2

original = C()
proxy = CProxy(original)

print(type(proxy))
# <class '__main__.CProxy'>
print(proxy.inner())
# 9
print(proxy.outer())
# <class '__main__.C'>
# 7
```

Thanks for responding @agronholm. I dropped _flask-apscheduler_ and am now using _APScheduler_ directly. I am able to replicate the issue. I've done some debugging and have found a deadlock caused in relation to `_jobstores_lock`.

After starting APScheduler a thread is spun off which executes `_process_jobs`, this sets the `_jobstores_lock` here: https://github.com/agronholm/apscheduler/blob/cbf2eeb21695343c1996e59732adbc8fbbab6842/apscheduler/schedulers/base.py#L929

A query executed within the context of this lock by a function called `get_due_jobs` never returns. I followed the execution down to the last executed line and it seems there is an issue receiving a new cursor from SQLAlchemy's connection pool (https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/master/lib/sqlalchemy/pool.py#L970).

Simultaneously, while the ^^^ request hangs, the main thread executes `add_job` which also requests a lock from `_jobstores_lock`. That lock request occurs here: https://github.com/agronholm/apscheduler/blob/cbf2eeb21695343c1996e59732adbc8fbbab6842/apscheduler/schedulers/base.py#L428

So I just started delving into the backend, and I can share what I know with you. If you wanted to know the exact lines of code, you can find the relevant code in chatterbot.generate_response method. Basically, when you setup your chatbot instance, you should setup the logic adapters you want to use:

```
bot = ChatBot('Ava',
    logic_adapters=[
        {
            "import_path": "chatterbot.logic.BestMatch",
	    "statement_comparison_function": "chatterbot.comparisons.levenshtein_distance",
            "response_selection_method": "chatterbot.response_selection.get_first_response"
        }
    ],
    filters=[
        'chatterbot.filters.RepetitiveResponseFilter'
    ]
)
```

In the above snippet, I use Levenshtein Distance for statement comparison, but I also use the Best Match algorithm for lookups, as well a last adapter for response selection. These logic adapters will be used after the preprocessor code (trim white space, escape chars, anything else you added) is executed.

Hope this helps!

To test performance on a Linux system:

```shell
# Make the caches
python3 make_single_cache.py
sudo python3 make_cache.py  # Needs to write caches in system directories

# Loading with no caches
sync ; echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches  # Simulate cold start
time python3 entrypoints.py

# Loading with per-directory caches
sync ; echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches  # Simulate cold start
time python3 read_cache.py

# Loading with single cache
sync ; echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches  # Simulate cold start
time python3 read_single_cache.py
```

By default the ``setup.py`` will look for ``lzma.h`` under ``$HOME/include/lzma.h`` so the simple answer is put this under your home folder rather than under "Program Files". ```

```

Or for 64 bit:

Hi :wink: This is not really a bug. `extra_css` is used internally to apply new styles to specific pages (eg. pages containing forms). I wouldn't really recommend to use the machina's `_base.html` template. Why don't you use the base template of your own application?

The way I see it, I'd override `board_base.html` template by ensuring it can properly fit inside my own application's template and I'd ensure that it extends my own `base.html` template

`_base.html` and `board_base.html` template are just base templates allowing you to quickly get started with *django-machina*, but you're encouraged to override them in order to properly embed forum templates into your application.

**That said**, the presence of `css`/`extra_css`/`js`/`extra_js` blocks does not really encourage that. Perhaps we should opt for new block names (more specific to *django-machina*): `machina_css`, `machina_extra_css`, `machina_js` and `machina_extra_js`. What do you think?

@bflad I was able to reproduce getting the RequestLimitExceeded error on my personal account by running five sessions in parallel, each doing a tight loop over ``describe_vpcs()``. However, using boto3 1.2.3 and botocore 1.3.30, this request is being properly retried with a random sleep, and eventually returns the proper response without raising an exception.

The only thing that I can think at this point is that your account is so far past its request limit that even with the default of five retries, it's still not succeeding. Unfortunately botocore simply raises the error to the caller if the maximum number of retries is surpassed.

There are two paths forward that I can see:
(1) you could install awslimitchecker from the [no-suppress-logging](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/tree/no-suppress-logging) branch and then run it with ``-vv``, which will include the full boto3 and botocore debug-level logging. That would allow me to confirm whether it's actually being retried or not. Unfortunately, when the maximum number of retries is exhausted, botocore simply raises the exception; aside from debug-level logging, there's no other way to confirm that this is the case.
(2) Per [this comment](https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/882#issuecomment-210631172), you can try copying botocore's [data/_retry.json](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/_retry.json) from your virtualenv to ``~/.aws/models/_retry.json`` and change ``max_attempts`` to a higher number. Unfortunately, per https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/882 there's currently no clearer way to do this.

However, my gut reaction to this is that if you're still seeing the exception after five retries with exponential backoff, there's something seriously wrong with requests going to your account - maybe some very badly-behaved client?

Hello @Sergevia,

it's true that the date filter is outdated. 
I looked a bit into it and found two things.
1. The datepicker in this example use `datepicker` as a directive but this is deprecated.
It is `uib-datepicker` now.
2. The angular team changed something with the Filter 'filter'. Inside the custom filter is a parameter with the name `expected`. In the old example it contains a object with 'before' and 'after' but since 1.3.6 of Angular it is a string.
From the Angular 1.3.6 [changelog](https://github.com/angular/angular.js/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md)

> Named properties in the expression object will only match against properties on the same level. Previously, named string properties would match against properties on the same level or deeper.

possible solution:
```
import platform

os_symlink = getattr(os, "symlink", None)
if callable(os_symlink):
    pass
elif platform.system() == 'Windows':

    def symlink_win(src, dst):
        import ctypes
        flags = 1 if src is not None and os.path.isdir(src) else 0
        try:
            dst = dst.replace('/', '\\')
            csl = ctypes.windll.kernel32.CreateSymbolicLinkW
            csl.argtypes = (ctypes.c_wchar_p, ctypes.c_wchar_p, ctypes.c_uint32)
            ret = csl(dst, src, flags)
        except Exception as e:
            pass
        if ret == 0:
            raise OSError

    os.symlink = symlink_win
else:
    pass

To build with amalgamation:
```bash
python3 -m pyroaring install --user
```

It will add an entry in your shell configuration file. Then, you have to apply the changes by either opening a new terminal or using `source`:
```bash
source ~/.zshrc
```

I did not test the installation on a mac, I would be happy to have any feedback.

The installation script can be found [here](https://github.com/Ezibenroc/PyRoaringBitMap/blob/master/pyroaring/__main__.py). In short, it firstly clones CRoaring repository, then does the following:
```bash
bash amalgamation.sh
gcc -march=native -O3 -std=c11 -shared -o libroaring.so -fPIC roaring.c
```

Just to reiterate what others have said here, explicitly checking the `X-Forwarded-Proto` here should not be needed and may cause confusion when parts of the application appear to be secure and others do not.

- When not behind a proxy, the header should be harmlessly irrelevant or potentially incorrect.
- When behind a proxy, `ProxyFix` should be used to automatically use this header when returning `request.is_secure`.
- If `ProxyFix` is not used and Flask-SSLify properly detects that the site is being served over HTTPS via `X-Forwarded-Proto`, links that require HTTPS (e.g. password reset links) may be generated using HTTP instead resulting in unwanted information leaks / security holes.

If this change would be accepted, I'm happy to make it and add some documentation regarding proxies to accompany it.

You can't add pycrypto as an external lib, you need to enable it on your appspot: https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/tools/libraries27

Note that you may need to convert the .p12 key file to a .pem to work on app engine. See [this issue](https://github.com/tylertreat/BigQuery-Python/issues/34) for more information.

That said, the recommended way to use this on App Engine is to *not* use a private key at all but instead to use `AppAssertionCredentials`.

```python
from bigquery import get_client
from bigquery import BIGQUERY_SCOPE
from oauth2client.appengine import AppAssertionCredentials

credentials = AppAssertionCredentials(scope=BIGQUERY_SCOPE)
get_client('my-project', credentials=credentials)
```

Turns out this 'fix' works by creating a sqlite3 file named `':memory:?cache=shared'` on disk. Apparently to pass uri options for the connection one needs to pass `uri=True` to `sqlite3.connect`, and the `uri` kwarg doesn't exist before python 3.4.

The only other solution that I can think of that would allow the use of an in-memory database would be to pass `check_same_thread=True` to `sqlite3.connect` and actually share the connection handle itself between threads, with locking, which would be a great big hassle.

I am assuming that I ran into this issue with `BooleanField`. My issue is that the field state can still be modified in the form, so the user doesn't understand that the field is read only.

The rendered html for current checkbox is 
```
<input checked id="test_field" name="test_field" readonly type="checkbox" value="y">
```
For some [odd reason](http://stackoverflow.com/a/155301) setting `readonly`attribute doesn't prevent changing the checkbox field state. So we could fix this by adding `disabled` attribute to the html like:
```
<input checked disabled id="test_field" name="test_field" readonly type="checkbox" value="y">
```

The same modification should also be done to the radio buttons, and possibly to some others as well.

Assuming that we want to show the user that the field can't be modified we now have two options:
 1. Make all readonly fields also disabled
 2. Create list of fields that should also be disabled when readonly is set
 3. Create separate function to add `disabled` attribute to fields, use it with these odd fields

I think that the readonly modification in the python code can also add the `disabled` attribute, so I would go with the option 1. I think it would be the easiest option, and it would hide the html oddness from the application code.

Adding `disabled` attribute does have [some effects](http://stackoverflow.com/a/7730719). I have found two small changes that come from adding the `disabled` attribute to all fields, but I think we can live with these:
 1. User can't see the potential choices in disabled select box
 2. User cant select text from disabled text input.

Hmm, this should be correct.
To be exact, that version of `get_slug` will return the slug for whatever language the instance has been loaded with. If not language is loaded, it will attempt to load current language (from `django.utils.translation.get_language()`).

What could go wrong here:
* If the instance already has a language loaded, and it's not the one you want.
* If the language from `get_language()` is incorrect. For instance because django's LocaleMiddleware is not loaded.
* If you expect a fallback chain. That is, if `get_language()` returns "fr-be", hvad will only try that exact language, it will not try "fr" unless you explicitly tell it to.

You can also see odd behaviors in the following conditions:
* If you cache objects between requests. The cached objects will not be reloaded, so they will stick to the old language.
* If you change current language in your view. If you do this, it is important to remember that current language is evaluated at the time the object is actually loaded. For queries, this means when the query is resolved by Django, not when you create the query.

Either way, in your template you access `city.mycity.slug`, which will be very slow as is, because neither `mycity` nor `slug` have been cached, so each item will cause two additional queries. You should prefetch them like this:

```python
# Use this if CityTranslated is a TranslatableModel:

context['favcities'] = (
    CityTranslated.objects.language()
                          .filter(country=country)
                          .select_related('mycity')
                          .order_by('-population')[:80]
)

# Use this if CityTranslated is NOT  a TranslatableModel:

mycity_qs = MyCity.objects.language()

context['favcities'] = (
    CityTranslated.objects.filter(country=country)
                          .prefetch_related(Prefetch('mycity', queryset=mycity_qs))
                          .order_by('-population')[:80]
)
```
Note: `Prefetch` can be imported from `django.db.models`.

The SCHEDULER_JOB_DEFAULTS configuration is per job, that means each job can execute at most 3 threads at the same time.

The SCHEDULER_EXECUTORS is a global configuration, in this case, only 1 thread will be used for all the jobs.

I believe the best way for you is to use max_workers: 1 when running locally

I see, if you don't want the fields to be serialized at all then my suggestion doesn't make much sense. 

> I can't imagine a situation where we could re-use the same serializer instance for an input context

Yeah you're right, since the serialiser is bound to the request then I can't think of a real practical case where you may want to reuse the instance. I guess that's just me not liking so much the fact serialisers in DRF are objects instead of functions with no state / side-effects.

I did actually look at this previously, and did not find an acceptable way.  The reason is that `QueryFields` is associated with a _serializer_, not with a _view_.  It is true that it works with `ModelSerializer` (and this is the typical use-case intended), but the `ModelSerializer` itself doesn't even access the db - in DRF, it gets passed a model instance, and uses plain old attribute lookup on the model instance.  

Note also that the code currently works with any plain old `Serializers` on apps that don't even have a database backend at all.  

The actual query that happens in DRF is not the responsibility of the serializer, but rather of the view (see [here](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/8f6173cd8afadd503dde124a616355ea80b5f6fe/rest_framework/generics.py#L73)).  So for `QueryFields` to influence the way data is pulled from the DB, it would have to 

- first determine if any fields were `ModelField` instances (easy).  
- introspect the view object somehow (probably possible but might involve black magic) 
- modify how the `get_queryset` method of the view works, dynamically, without breaking anything that DRF relies on internally (sounds fiendishly difficult)

In the end I decided this could not be the responsibility of `QueryFields`, and would be better suited as a feature request directly for DRF's `ModelSerializer`.

2) Regarding the code changes, I did two minor changes and kept the main logic same as current version.
   
      - only do the modulus calculation to `ret_indices` instead of full `indices` after calling `TopkSort()` function, which could reduce many redundant calculation when `k` is smaller than element number. So I changed `indices = F<mshadow_op::mod>(indices, element_num);`  to `ret_indices = F<mshadow_op::mod>(ret_indices, element_num);`. That's why the results from `ret_type=[value, indices, both]` shows better than `ret_type=mask`.
      - in the case of `axis=-1`, there's no need to do the `transpose` to the source(input) data, but only requires a flatten to 1D operation. `FlatTo1D` is much efficient than `reshape` since it only changes the Shape but not result in data movement. I noticed that in current CPU `TopkSort()` function, the `work` Tensor is only used to stored the temporary result and then copy its content back to `sorted_data`. If we pass the flattened data via `work` Tensor to `TopkSort()`, and copy the top k number of data from such flattened data into `sorted_data` directly, then there's no need to keep additional temporal Tensor and do the copy second time. For the case `axis!=-1`, both transpose and reshape are required to convert a 3D Tensor to 1D Tensor. That's why the results from `axis=-1` are much better than other cases.

Hope this answers your question, I could add some comments in the code if there's no other concerns.

You can try Gluon API, which is more flexible for such kind of complicate network.
A simple sketch example for your network:
```python
class CNNLSTM(HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, ...):
        self.cnn_block = ...
        self.lstm_block = mxnet.gluon.rnn.LSTM(...)
        self.fc_block = ...

    def hybrid_forward(F, x):
        y1 = self.cnn_block(x)
        y2 = self.lstm_block(y1)
        y3 = F.concat(y1, y2)
        z = self.fc_block(y3)
        return z
```

I cannot comment for using the core MKL library, but to use the MKL2017 and  MKL2017 Experimental you will need to do the following: 
1. Build the mxnet library with USE_MKL2017 = 1 and USE_MKL2017_EXPERIMENTAL = 1
2. Prior to make the R library (sudo make rpkg), create static links to the MKL libraries: 
```
ln -s /usr/local/lib/libiomp5.so /lib/libiomp5.so
ln -s /usr/local/lib/libmklml_gnu.so /lib/libmklml_gnu.so \
ln -s /usr/local/lib/libmklml_intel.so /lib/libmklml_intel.so
```
The above works seemlessly, building process take cares of downloading the MKL2017 libraries. 

Not sure of the added benefits of using MKL instead of openblas if the MKL2017 and Experimental and enabled, but you'll need to modify some parameters in the `make/config.mk`, look also at: `MKL_README.md`

While investigating this also updating the gunicorn version could solve this issue.

Gunicorn [`19.5.0`](https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/releases/tag/19.5.0) introduced a alternative to `--forwarded-allow-ips` config, where you can define a environment variable `FORWARDED_ALLOW_IPS` to override the default `"127.0.0.1"` value. http://docs.gunicorn.org/en/stable/settings.html#forwarded-allow-ips.

Even further in [`19.7.0`](https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/releases/tag/19.7.0) there is `GUNICORN_CMD_ARGS` that could be used to pass arguments to the running gunicorn.

This is something that could be investigated as an alternative option as there is a [PR to support the gunicorn versions `> 19.4.0`](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/2237)

If you use `development-js` `development-css` etc in your `manifest.cfg` it will add a diazo bundle to the content. So you can then have your theme with all of the plone patterns (eg: the toolbar).

However, when viewing a backend page we don't want the front end theme displayed, so this PR is to remove the diazo bundles in `backend.xml`. 

The logic here is that if somebody was including the backend in their `rules.xml`, this would be the behaviour they expect. For example, the theme might make css changes to forms which breaks the control panel views.

>This seems very taskcluster specific, when I think we want this pulse schema to be generic to anyone. That said, job guids are something that we've talked about trying to get rid of, so not sure what the best approach is here.

Well, treeherder/sheriffs wants to know about infrastructure retries. In that case I want future treeherder to be one that can knows not just that "a job was retried", I should have the relational information and know what job it is retried in.

I previously proposed introducing `"retryOf": <other_job_guid>`, so a job could declare that it was a retry of a previous job. Hence, `<other_job_guid>` is declared retried. That would be equally okay to me.

But I don't like submitting a message saying "job <job_guid> is retried", TH needs to preserve the relationship between "old retried job", and "new job trying again".
Because then TH will never get to state where it only shows the job once and hides the retries by default.  And ideally infrastructure retries should not be something people concern themselves with :)

Thanks, so the documentation is a little bit scattered across their website so here are two links that allow the understanding of AWS_SESSION_TOKEN (I'll summarize here)

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_temp.html
This page gives an overview of what temporary credentials are. They are generated by the STS service to give access to resources for a limited amount of time. When queried, it returns a temporary `access key id`, `security id` as well a `session token` that are valid for a predefined amount of time. (See [here](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/s3.py#L115) for how sts is queried in Luigi) Now to use temporary credentials, the session token must be passed to the `S3Connection` constructor.

See boto documentation as well, they mention how SESSION_TOKEN is mandatory when using temporary credentials.
http://boto.cloudhackers.com/en/latest/ref/sts.html?highlight=SESSION%20TOKEN#boto.sts.credentials.Credentials


Because the aws_session_token variable is forced to be None in the current code, we are only left with one option in terms of temporary credentials; ask luigi to assume the role. And even with that, it wouldn't work if the sts:assumeRole requires MFA identification (this can be another MR potentially)

The scenario that I'm trying to allow here is for those who store the temporary credentials as env variables (a lot of production code really).

As to the naming convention, it is unfortunate but I do not think there is really anything we can do about that because the attribute names are generated from the response element names. import os

                                          Body=fh)
finally:
    shutil.rmtree(dirname)
```

> Using fromworkdir involves writing it to disk twice: first to the working directory, and then to the objects database.

Using `_fromchunks()` is the same. First we have to write to a temporary file and then to the object database (they both use the same function to perform the work).

It is not possible to write create an object without knowing the size ahead of time. Anything which does not ask for the size beforehand (or for a full buffer) must store the data somewhere else before writing to the odb.

Python already has ways of making arbitrary sources look like a buffer/stream, so I'd rather use a readable stream interface rather than callbacks.

@bpicolo Yes, they may be listed as exceptions, each and every one of those is a valid response too. As the `pyramid_tm` changelog notes:

> This means it's best to "raise HTTPFound(...)" instead of "return HTTPFound(...)" if you want an HTTP exception to abort the transaction.

Your changes means that if I return an `HTTPNotFound()` which previously would have simply caused the transaction to be committed, it no longer will with your PR, since it is a 400 class error message.

It is well documented in Pyramid that returning an `HTTPException` will simply render it as a response, whereas raising it will instead cause the exception machinery to kick in, and ultimately caught by the default exception view and rendered. `pyramid_tm` inserts itself in between so that it can correctly roll-back the transaction.

One method of debugging is to place the following line of code,
```python
 import pdb,sys; pdb.Pdb(stdout=sys.__stdout__).set_trace()
```
into the source code which on windows is usually found C:\Python2x\lib\site-packages\Selenium2Library\...

This will allow you to run the [Python pdb debugger](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pdb.html) at the command prompt.

Python has traditionally distinguish between library packaging and application packaging. The former uses `setup.py` (and friends) to configure a package when it is downloaded from PyPI; the latter uses `requirements.txt`. A project can be both at the same time, but it’s up to you to create configurations for both scenarios and sync them correctly (if you want to). Pipenv as things stand handles application packaging, with Pipfile replacing `requirements.txt`. It doesn’t not handle library packaging and uploading to PyPI (and can’t; `Pipfile` lacks metadata to create a PyPI package).

This, discussion in #1263, and [PEP 518](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/) give me a project idea though. What if there is a pipeline that

1. Takes information from `Pipfile` and `pyproject.toml`
2. Creates a `setup.py` on the fly
3. Package (`setup.py sdist` etc.)
4. Upload to PyPI (using [twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine))

I’ll experiment a bit when I have time and report back if there’s progress 😎

pip doesn't treat ``--extra-index-url`` differently from ``--index-url``, the only difference is ``--index-url`` overwrites the default and ``--extra-index-url`` adds an additional one, but beyond that they are exactly the same. So ``Pipfile`` will treat it just like any other source.

The issue here is that the meaning of `pypi` has changed to point to the new host. Simply setting a repository in the `[pypi]` section was never intended to override the default repository setting.

I recommend you adapt your project no longer to use the register command and simply to upload the package to the new PyPI.

If you must continue to use the old PyPI, you may do so by customizing your dev environment and projects to support the old pypi. For example in `.pypirc`:

```
[oldpypi]
repository=https://pypi.python.org/pypi
...
```

And in each project's setup.cfg:

```
[register]
repository=oldpypi
```

However, I recommend instead just using the latest setuptools and reconfiguring your projects not to register.

To respond to some of your feedback:

I'm sure this is going to have regressions as well. Hopefully the parallel release will help uncover most of them before we push this out to everyone.

This actually doesn't prevent it from being usable without installing. You can run ``python -m virtualenv`` from within the git clone and it'll work just fine. That's what I was doing prior to the ``setup.py`` getting fixed. In fact I think this makes it a little bit cleaner because instead of having to keep track of ``virtualenv.py`` **and** ``virtualenv_support/`` you now only have to keep track of ``virtualenv/``. Although one question I've been toying with is whether or not we want to make it possible for other people to register builders or not. If we do then I think it'd make sense to use entry points to enable that, however I'm having a hard time coming up with an actual use case for that which isn't better covered by telling someone to backport or fix the ``venv`` module (primarily it's about different implementations wanting to provide their own builder). If we really think single file is important we could also ship an executable zip file on ``bootstrap.pypa.io`` similar to ``get-pip.py`` except that acts as a stand alone virtualenv file.

As far as having dependencies goes, I'm still on the fence. I don't think click brings massive wins for virtualenv, but I do think it could help clean up some of pip. Part of using it here was a (hopeful) future unification of just using click for both pip and virtualenv as well as a test bed for seeing how well click actually works out for us. That being said I'm not completely married to the idea either however there are two possible solutions to the "we don't want dependencies" view point in virtualenv that don't include dropping dependencies. The first is to simply do what pip does and vendor it, I'm not a super big fan of this option because unlike pip virtualenv doesn't really have a great reason for not wanting dependencies other than just a desire not to have them. The second one is basically the same thing as above, we can ship an executable zip file on bootstrap.pypa.io which people can use for a "self contained" virtualenv executable and just have the version on PyPI act as the "I'm going to manage this with a package manager" version.

Greetings. I am one of the creators of YAML. Tabs are not allowed in indentation and an error should happen when they are detected in a place where indentation might occur.

IMHO libyaml is the most perfect implementation of the YAML 1.1 spec (which is very close to YAML 1.2).

Here is the result of the OP test in Perl's libyaml binding: https://gist.github.com/ac5821875eb30979f7a8
Here is the same program in Python: https://gist.github.com/582ea40dbdcd473249a4

They both throw the same error. I believe it is a wise choice to test arguable behaviors against a libyaml binding. Possibly even moreso than the spec.

FWIW, we spent every day for 3 months (circa 2003) trying to sanely allow tabs into YAML indentation. We came to the conclusion that it was an insane endeavor. (It ends up being much harder than, say, Python indentation based scoping).

I use a custom DRF field for this:

### models.py

```python

from enum import Enum
from django.db import models
from enumfields import EnumIntegerField

class GeoModel(models.Model):

    class LocationType(Enum):
        ROOFTOP = 1
        RANGE_INTERPOLATED = 2
        GEOMETRIC_CENTER = 3
        APPROXIMATE = 4
        UNRESOLVED = 5

    location_type = EnumIntegerField(
        enum=LocationType,
        default=LocationType.UNRESOLVED
    )
    ...
```

### fields.py

```python
from rest_framework import serializers


class EnumField(serializers.ChoiceField):
    def __init__(self, enum, **kwargs):
        self.enum = enum
        kwargs['choices'] = [(e.name, e.name) for e in enum]
        super(EnumField, self).__init__(**kwargs)

    def to_representation(self, obj):
        return obj.name

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        try:
            return self.enum[data]
        except KeyError:
            self.fail('invalid_choice', input=data)

```

### serializers.py
```python

from rest_framework import serializers
from . import fields
from . import models

class GeoModelSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):

    class Meta:
        model = models.GeoModel

    location_type = fields.EnumField(enum=models.GeoModel.LocationType)
```

@aodiwei As far as I can tell this is the expected behavior. It's happening due to the use of weak references in the topics dictionary that are not initialized until a call to `__getitem__` on that dictionary. In python 2.7 on my laptop, calling `print(client.topics)` shows the same output you're getting, presumably (and I'm guessing here) because python 2.7's `print()` does not call `__getitem__` on dictionary arguments.

I can see that this is confusing for more than just a few users, so it's worth considering how to make this interface more readable. Maybe we override `TopicDict.__repr__` to include a note about weak references? We might just make the documentation a bit clearer as well.

I just took a quick look at this, and it looks like although the name of the file is used here in order to add multipart's `filename` parameter [according to RFC2388](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2388.txt), as indicated by that same, providing that parameter is optional and not really necessary.

Actually, there's a small issue with the PR in its current state. CI tests work because Travis environments for 2.6 and 3.3 come pre-installed with a pytest version that is still compatible with these Python versions, so running `python setup.py test ...` will use the preinstalled pytest (3.0.3).

However, when using a clean environment, `python setup.py test ...` will download the latest pytest version (3.3.1 currently) which is no longer compatible with 2.6 and 3.3 (cf. https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/changelog.html#pytest-3-3-0-2017-11-23).

We could fix this with a bit of code in `setup.py` (or just by freezing the pytest version to 3.2.5, the latest compatible version), but I think we should consider dropping support for these Python versions entirely. I have a few other improvements that I'd like to ship too but that require a major version bump. Happy to discuss this further internally.

    '1.0.dev456',

    '1.0a12',

    '1.0+5',
    '1.0.post456.dev34',
    '1.0.post456',
    '1.1.dev1',
]

def make_semver(version_str):
    v = parse_version(version_str)
    major = v._version.release[0]
    try:
        minor = v._version.release[1]
    except IndexError:
        minor = 0
    try:
        patch = v._version.release[2]
    except IndexError:
        patch = 0
    
    prerelease = []
    if v._version.pre:
        prerelease.append("".join(str(x) for x in v._version.pre))
    if v._version.dev:
        prerelease.append("".join(str(x) for x in v._version.dev))
    prerelease = ".".join(prerelease)
    
    local = v.local
    
    version = "{0}.{1}.{2}".format(major, minor, patch)
    
    if prerelease:
        version += "-{0}".format(prerelease)
    if local:
        version += "+{0}".format(local)
    
    return version


for t in tests:
    make_semver(t)

> But what the last report number is, for example 123 and suddenly the number 003 is free and the next paper get this number 003 ?

This is not what I meant. The counter will keep going, so the next after ``123`` will be ``124``. However, say that something happened, so that ``124`` wasn't used, hence next report number will be ``125``. You then have a user saying, I want to use number ``124`` because I don't want holes in the sequence + you don't want to have to mingle with the counter. In this case, you can still create a report number with number 124, because PIDstore have your back and can tell you that report number ``124`` was never actually minted. 

Short story long - sequence generator can focus on generating a sequence. PIDStore keeps track of every report number ever minted.

One way of looking at it is that including the interface in hashing *is* closer to what zope.schema used to do: hashing was identity based, so `hash(IOne['one']) != hash(ITwo['two'])` (and hashing only worked on Python 2, so anyone relying on hashing could not port to Python 3). But now that hashing works on Python 3, and is consistent with equality, `hash(IOne['one']) == hash(ITwo['two'])`. Previously on Python 2, a `dict` could include both `IOne['one']` and `ITwo['two']`:

```pycon
>>> d = {}
>>> d[IOne['one']] = 'one'
>>> d[ITwo['two']] = 'two'
>>> d
{<zope.schema._bootstrapfields.Int at 0x10fe64ed0>: 'one',
 <zope.schema._bootstrapfields.Int at 0x10ffdb610>: 'two'}
```

Now it cannot:
```pycon
>>> d = {}
>>> d[IOne['one']] = 'one'
>>> d[ITwo['two']] = 'two'
>>> d
{<zope.schema._bootstrapfields.Int at 0x10bc25d90>: 'two'}
```

Including the interface in equality and hashing would restore the ability to have both fields in a dict, while staying true to the language spec.

From your reply on #kivy, it sounds like your problem was that will_build returns a function. This is deliberate, this function is for passing conditions to recipe builds in the depends list, not for what you're trying.

The reason is that you have the `console_scripts` entry point pointing at a class, rather than a function. `setuptools` generates a shim for each entry point that calls its corresponding function and then passes the returned value to `sys.exit()`. Because the entry point is a class, `sys.exit()` is passed the value returned by `ToolchainCL()`, which is the `ToolchainCL` object. `sys.exit()` then takes the non-`None` value [as an error message and prints it](https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.exit).

[The fix](http://www.scotttorborg.com/python-packaging/command-line-scripts.html) is to move most of `ToolchainCL.__init__()` to some `main()` function, and set that as the `console_scripts` entry point.

Just to offer a perspective from within the LSST Simulations team:

@dkirkby is correct.  The current implementation of the CatSim-GalSim interface does use the cameraGeom software provided by the DM afw package to take positions on the sky and transform them into positions on the camera and on individual detectors.  There are some advantages and disadvantages to this.

Advantages:
1) The framework is actually LSST-agnostic.  afw.cameraGeom is a software package that reads in specifications from a text file (or a FITS file, or any other number of input formats) and creates a self-consistent model of all the coordinate transformations associated with a sky-to-camera system.  In addition to detector layout, this also includes pincushion and (I think) Zernike distortions.  Right now, the API is a little cumbersome, but we are working on python scripts that will make it fairly easy for users to generate an arbitrary camera layout using a simple text file outlining the positions and sizes of chips and pixes.

2) Because of (1), if the engineering team ever changes the specs on the LSST camera, the software will automatically be in sync (assuming it gets updated with the newest distribution of the relevant packages).  We do not need to worry about passing around GalSim-specific text files every time something changes at LSST.

Disadvantages:
1) afw is a C++ package that takes about 45 minutes to compile on a modern laptop and occupies 216M of disk space.

2) (1) isn't that bad, but afw brings with it dependencies on a lot of other, unnecessary DM software packages.  The Simulations team would definitely like to split cameraGeom off from all of this baggage and make it much more portable.  That is a conversation that will have to involve the DM team, since they are more aware of how feasible such a scheme is.

If continuing down this path does not seem palatable, other options are:

1) Write a new python package that re-implements the functionality of afw.cameraGeom without pulling in all of the unnecessary dependencies.  As I said, the LSST Simulations team is not terribly pleased with our own dependence on afw, so we are open to writing a new package which the LSST Simulations stack will use to implement camera geometry.  We can hand that package over to GalSim so that, at least, GalSim and LSST Simulations will remain in synch.  This has the disadvantage that we will have to make sure that sims.cameraGeom and afw.cameraGeom remain in sync, but that is sort of in our (LSST Simulations) job description, anyway.

2) GalSim implements its own LSST camera module and we (LSST Simulations) become responsible for updating the inputs to that code whenever the specifications for the LSST camera change.  There's a lot of risk for things to get out of sync with this scheme.  My preference would be to come up with a single software package that both GalSim and LSST Simulations depend on for simulating the LSST camera.

We can talk about this in person next week during the WL2 session.  This is where my/our (I'm not 100% sure who all I speak for) thinking is at the moment.

>  It seems like these could be enabled with a flag exclude_marginalmaybe? We already have 
> exclude_fail and exclude_bad which presumably remove the more extreme bad objects. 

An alternate way to do this might be to have a single flag called something like ```exclusion_level``` which has several options: `marginal', `bad', `failures' (or whatever).  I think that in general there will be little use for combinations like ```exclude_marginal=True``` and ```exclude_fail=False```, so having a single flag with multiple options could be the simplest way to cover all the cases that people might use in reality.

I would definitely distinguish between these kinds of cuts that are meant to single out issues in postage stamp generation or fit quality, vs. the SNR and resolution cuts that were used for GREAT3 (I think it's really just those two that go in the second category).

I think it ought to be possible to refactor the core of cameraGeom into a lightweight package that GalSim, LSST Sims, and DM could all depend on.  DM's cameraGeom in afw would then just provide interoperability with other afw classes.

The main difficulty is that DM will need access to at least some functionality in C++, and we don't presently have any C++ code in the stack that delegates to Python.  We've been thinking about doing that, so it's not out of the question, but it might make this project a bit harder if it's the first such case.  Another option would be to implement at least some of the lightweight camera package in C++ as well, but not necessarily use Swig or the any of the DM approach to providing Python bindings (e.g. you could use Boost.Python, Cython, or the raw Python C API - whichever was easiest for this problem).  DM could then depend on the C++ interface from the lightweight package and then either add its own Python bindings or find a way to make the lightweight package's bindings work with Swig.

Je déterre cette issue, qui repose problème dans le merge de la version IPP d'OpenFisca avec la version master.

Aujourd'hui cohabitent dans le modèle de la législation française deux représentations des revenus non salariaux.

Par exemple:

- `rag` et `tns_benefice_exploitant_agricole` représentent la même chose
- `microentreprise` et `tns_micro_entreprise_benefice` représentent la même chose, sauf que `microentreprise` est agrégée à l'échelle du foyer fiscal.
- `tns_micro_entreprise_chiffre_affaires` correspond à `mbic_impv`, `mbic_imps` ou `mbnc_impo` suivant la valeur de `tns_micro_entreprise_type_activite`
- `tns_auto_entrepreneur_chiffre_affaires` correpond à la valeur détaillée par mois de `ebic_impv`, `ebic_imps` ou `ebnc_impo` suivant la valeur de `tns_auto_entrepreneur_type_activite`
- `tns_autres_revenus` correspond à tous les autres types de revenus non salariés. Mais pour les prestations sociales, on a besoin de savoir de quel type d'activité il s'agit (`tns_autres_revenus_type_activite`), ainsi que le CA de l'entreprise (`tns_autres_revenus_chiffre_affaires`)

Comme disait @benjello, il serait bon ramener une certaines cohérences en fusionnant les doublons et/ou en les liant par des formules.

Il me semble aussi qu'il y a eu ces dernières années une fusion entre les régimes de la micro entreprise et de l'auto-entreprise. Mais je ne suis pas sûr des impacts réels sur notre modèle (est-ce qu'aujourd'hui les seuls bénéficiaires du régime microfiscal sont les micro-entrepreneurs qui déclare leur activité au trimestre ?).
Obsolète.

Ok, that makes sense.  I changed the default index to use `model._meta.pk.name` instead of `"id"`.

The indexes not showing up in JSON output is an unintended side effect of using `orient="records"` with pandas' `to_json()` function.  The index is useful for almost every other format, for example the Excel output which puts the index as the first column and bold.  There isn't a JSON orient that does exactly what we want, but there is an open ticket to add one (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/5729).

For now, I came up with a custom DRP-specific orient, `"records-index"`, which resets the index before rendering the dataframe.  I made this the default.

Why are we not getting 100%?  (It is not a bug in the coverage calculation):

Solidity compiles a check for the size of the input data
```
*  28: CALLDATASIZE
*  29: PUSH1 0x0
*  2b: DUP2
*  2c: DUP2
*  2d: LT
*  2e: ISZERO
*  2f: ISZERO
*  30: PUSH2 0x35
*  33: JUMPI
   34: INVALID
*  35: JUMPDEST
```
And we are not trying different data sizes (notably not a zero length) so 100% coverage is not 
 actually achievable.

Let me make one more appeal for the way I originally did this. With my approach, it's a very simple rule:

* To create a Python object that maps to an API resource, call the lowercase name of the function on the `client` object

With your approach, the user is forced to know whether or not the API object your creating needs access to the client to accomplish its other methods. Sometimes they call the factory on the client with lowercase, sometimes they call it uppercase at the module level. The distinction is made purely based on an implementation detail, which is whether the object needs access to the `client` object to accomplish its other methods. Essentially you are exposing internal implementation details as part of the API. Consider what happens if we *do* need to add `client` to Metric or Resource? Now we need to break our API.  It also makes things trickier in the case of TimeSeries where we do want a factory function but don't need a reference to `client`.

I stick by my guns that's a primary use case for the staticmethod decorator.

Obviously its your repo and @rimey API so whatever conclusion you all come to is fine by me.

blob = bucket['/remote/path/to/file.txt']

>>> blob = Blob('/remote/path/to/file.txt')

True
```

@landrito pease note f5ca60c7

>  In our current implementation, a subscription with self.topic set to None will not be able to call Subscription.create() (there may be other methods that require a topic to be set on a Subscription but none that I can tell).

Right. I can see the logic there, since you always create a subscription to a topic.  But in my mind the right solution is to have a Client().create_subscription(topic=topic_name, subscription_name) or Client().topic(topic_name).create_subscription(subscription_name).   

`
// current logic
s = Subscription(topic_name, client=Client())
if not s.exists(): 
  s.create()
`

The alternative would be:
`
if !Client().subscription_exists(): // create subscription
s = Subscription(subscription_name) // always produces a fully working object
`
or at least
`
try:
  s = Subscription(subscription_name)
catch SubscriptionResourceDoesNotExistError:
  Client().topic(...).create_subscription(...)
`
We are getting into matters of taste, I think. 

> Can you help me understand when a Subscription should have a Topic and when it doesn't matter? 

The only use case I can think of is listing subscriptions to a given topic (rather than all subscriptions in the active project). This is, sort of, a convenience method. So the long form is:
` 
[i for i in Client().list_subscription() if i.topic_name = topic()]
`
The possibly neater version is 
`
Client.topic().get_subscriptions()
`

There are two distinct API methods. 

> Also, can you give an example of a "a subscription resource name to work with and not know the topic"?

If I want to give a third party developer in my project a minimum scope of access, I might give that person a subscription.pull role but not a topic.get permission.  This way, the developer may access the data but not modify the access points. (see https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/access_control)

For [posterity][1]

Here is a proof of concept for running readable interpreter snippets without worrying about calling `eval` / `exec` etc.

First, create a fake module (or any object really) with our content in the `__doc__`

```python
>>> import types
>>> fake_mod = types.ModuleType('haha-nope')
>>> fake_mod.__doc__ = """\
... Here is some prose, and then a dragon appears
... and it makes sure division::
...
... >>> a = 45
... >>> a / 3
... 15
...
... and then the bunny hops down the lane and we
... find out we have to print a null::
...
... >>> b = {}
... >>> v = b.get('anything')
... >>> v
... >>> print(v)
... None
... """
```

then hand that object off to `doctest.run_docstring_examples`

```python
>>> import doctest
>>> fake_globals = {}
>>> doctest.run_docstring_examples(fake_mod, fake_globals, verbose=False, name='foo')
>>> doctest.run_docstring_examples(fake_mod, fake_globals, verbose=True, name='foo')
Finding tests in foo
Trying:
    a = 45
Expecting nothing
ok
Trying:
    a / 3
Expecting:
    15
ok
Trying:
    b = {}
Expecting nothing
ok
Trying:
    v = b.get('anything')
Expecting nothing
ok
Trying:
    v
Expecting nothing
ok
Trying:
    print(v)
Expecting:
    None
ok
```
