I am adding the following safety measures as a mitigation effort:

1. cache_manager will clean and recreate results_dirs [for invalid targets](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/invalidation/cache_manager.py#L185)*
2. cache_manager will refuse to [update a vt to valid](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/invalidation/cache_manager.py#L264) if the results_dir is no longer a symlink
3. [relative_symlink](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/0039ddac382b40f26666e317766ce25b91be60c4/src/python/pants/util/dirutil.py#L279) will provide a first-class error message upon attempting to overwrite an existing directory

* useful for task correctness and, if known, removes the motivation to clean arbitrary results_dirs. But largely orthogonal to the issue at hand.

Hopefully those will mitigate the discovered issues. I personally believe that Pants will continue to experience pain from this issue while we are quietly passing a symlink instead of an actual dir.

My instinct is that it would be better to pass tasks the canonical link and coalesce all consumption to one canonical location for both products and caches. That would leave the symlinks as internal implementation details and we could more comfortably rely on the state.

If anyone has a motivating case for why passing the consumer a symlink is preferred, I would love the context.

Fixed in #1864

|                        | synced=true | synced=false |
| ------------------ |:------------------:| -----------------:|
| cached=true   | Client side model instantiated and is synced | Client side model instantiated and is not synced |
| cached=false  | Client side model removed from cache (but has been synced)      |   Client side model removed from cache (but has not been synced) |

The main reason for the distinction between 'synced' and 'cached' is that it allows us to cache bust a collection by virtue of its constituent models.

We could simplify this and just set the Model.synced to be false and have the Collection synced be a getter of all Model.synced.

It is possible that this could be resolved with browser caching - however, my fear is that the browser caching implementations are quite different, so we will have far less control over when the cache is invalidated (unless we do make an actual request at every fetch, and hence tie up the server with many more requests - even though they may be fairly light in cost as 304s).

#### When I try to execute a command like `make` on CodaLab, it fails because of "read-only" permissions. What do I do?

Each bundle uploaded to CodaLab is read-only. If a command you are running creates new files, you'll have to create a duplicate of the in the run command. For example, instead of running `cl run :my-bundle 'cd my-bundle && make`, run `cl run :my-bundle 'cp -r my-bundle duplicate && cd duplicate && make' -n duplicate`. The json.dumps approach is better since it preserves order for display.

Disadvantage as noted in the stackoverflow thread is that we lose the type information -- it prints as a dictionary. Here's an [interesting thread](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1131495/in-xml-is-order-important) about XML ordering (it sometimes matters).

We should consider these tradeoffs when using `json.dumps`:

- Ordering is implicit: it's not clear from the printed output that this is an ordered data structure
- Similarly, copy / paste from the terminal will lose ordering

`json.dumps` is more readable than using `OrderedDict.__repr__()`, and I think that issues from ordering ambiguity will be rare. However, it may be helpful to the user to have knowledge that the returned data type from Tapioca is ordered for their own programming purposes.

@ashaffer you have two options:

1) make all your code asynchronous, so you use asyncio or twisted style coding in one thread together with the websocket.

2) Put all the websocket code in its own thread and communicate with your threads using a push/pull queue like the already mentioned Janus (and I'm sure you can find something equivalent for twisted). In the Janus readme you can find a code example on how to communicate an asyncio thread (in your case the websocket thread) with other threads (in your case the rest of your program).

Note that in case 2 your program will still use only one core since the python GIL does not allow two threads to run at the same time. The difference is that in case 1 you decide in which order your code executes, while in case 2 the python thread scheduler will switch between the threads as its see fit, so it should be slightly less efficient.

This should solve your doubts, since I think we have already dirtied the issue with enough of topic.

Well, we can't have `txaio.create_future(loop=..)` because Twisted doesn't support that -- this is why `txaio` has *one* event-loop configured globally.

But, I guess you're suggesting some sort of intermediate object that gets passed a loop (and in Twisted case I guess it would just have to throw an exception if the loop isn't "the one true reactor"). e.g. something like `txaio.with_loop(loop)` or something, and the object that returns has all the API methods of `txaio` (`create_future`, etc).

I still ask, though: is there really a use-case for N loops? Or is "default loop or 1 explicit loop" sufficient? Can you point me to examples of asyncio code that use > 1 loop (and why they do that)? The use-case in this particular issue is for testing, which seems completely sufficient to have just *one* replacement loop.

Another possible solution:

 - use asyncio-specific code in this module (i.e. `Future(loop=)` directly
 - make sure that if *no* loop is passed (or None), we use `txaio.config.loop` (if *it's* not None)

This would, hopefully (and, should have tests!) mean that someone who never passed `loop=` but *did* use `txaio` to set an explicit loop would still get that txaio-known explicit loop but people who either currently use the `loop=` API or who need > 1 loop can do so. I still think this will lead to odd situations and weird edge-cases, though: any "common" code in the generic stuff will be using `txaio.*` and only see the one `txaio` loop (e.g. any code in `autobahn.websocket.protocol`).

Err, wat? No.
I mean, there are some choices:
1. `date_filed` is a DateTime in the local timezone of the court.
2. `date_filed` is a DateTime in a the standard timezone, Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)
3. `date_filed` is a DateTime in some other timezone, like `US/Pacific` where Mike lives

It seems clear to me that (1), which I suggested in the first comment, really doesn't make sense. It would mean, for instance, that if you did a group of queries across multiple cases in CL, you could not sensibly order the results based on the DateTime field.

It also seems to me that (3) is...just unwise. But I may be speaking with East Coast parochialism.

Of course, it seems like no matter which of these choices are chosen, the zone needs to be recorded. Does it need to be recorded once for every DateTime field? I fear that's the best practice.

Timezone experts will tell you that events should be stored in the zone they are referenced to, so that when the zone rules change it doesn't leave you with a mess. It's what lead to my suggesting (1). But that implies a database type that stores timezones and ~we don't have that~ -- but actually we do!

We set `USE_TZ = True` in Django settings, and Python's `datetime` tuples support timezones.
Except...the Python API for this is very confusing and I can't even figure out how to use it.

So...I don't know.
Other than I think (3) is right out, because [the Django docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/topics/i18n/timezones/) say:

> When support for time zones is enabled, Django stores datetime information in UTC in the database, uses time-zone-aware datetime objects internally, and translates them to the end user’s time zone in templates and forms.

So basically (2), except we need an additional zone field for every datetime field? Maybe? Probably? I don't even.

> Have you looked into how this is going to interact with the JournalDB or whether this should be integrated with that class in some way. If an internal call within a transaction touches some state and then those state changes are reverted due to an evm exception, do the touched parts still remain in the read/write list? My assumption is yes, but if not, we need to take that into account.

My assumption is that in the stateless client use case, `StateDB` is more like a **disposable** container because:
1. With pure function, we can use StateDB more flexibly.
2. We mainly need it for creating the `collation witness data`. So `reads` and `writes` data is no longer need for stateless client.
3. So if transaction fails, we just don't include the `reads` and `writes` data of this tx

>I have been thinking similarly about our opcodes. That all opcodes in `eth.vm.logic` should be moved into the `FrontierVM`

Mmh...I'm not so sure about that one. After all, these are logic functions that even private chains may want to use. We already *configure* the opcodes per fork. 

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/bf1d3c2ad653b4ed6ab9ffb836735863bb5d8148/eth/vm/forks/constantinople/opcodes.py#L24-L40

But imagine you are implementing a private chain and want to use `add`, `sub`, `mul` etc and have to import it from `eth.vm.forks.frontier`. That would feel weird, no?

I can see the point though, chances are another fork wants to implement `mul` differently (unlikely but anyway) and so we end up with a different implementation of `mul`. But even then, I think this second `mul` implementation would just end up living in `eth.vm.logic` as long as it is *generic* enough to be used across different forks and chains.

This PR discussed during 02/23/2018 weekly meeting.

The first issue was an intentional workaround for an issue where conda needed to pick the right version. I think the issue has been fixed, so it can probably be made to be what you would expect. 

The second issue is https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/915.

@asmeurer did you mean [these lines](https://github.com/asmeurer/dotfiles/blob/8bcba698cde26cf81e765d456dddc20cf0d1865f/.profile#L370-L384)? Seems like you may've changed your `.profile` so they don't seem to point somewhere reasonable
It does look like `bottleneck` requires `numpy 1.9` or later so that is certainly enough to explain the conflict. Agreed that the hint is cryptic; we are working on that.

conda-build is using conda's standard channel capability to "reveal" its packages to conda proper. It would be interesting if we could find a way to specify that a particular channel is "ephemeral", and therefore to always assume that the cached packages are stale.
No, because we're on a shared cluster so we don't have root access

I *really* would like conda to get to a place where we can have more concurrency in everything.  Both during the download-extract phase, and during the unlink-link phase.  What I envisioned when I created this ticket was a docker-esque progressbar.  I googled around a bit looking for the library docker uses for this.  It might be https://github.com/gosuri/uiprogress.  At least it's a good example.

I guess that turns out to be incredibly hard with python.  Because I haven't seen anything like it built yet.  Not even tqdm (which is what we're now using as of conda 4.4).
Multi-user installs are fully supported as of conda 4.3.

I would say that cloning currently is not reliable, at least I cannot rely on it so I prefer to do things by hand for now. Even using a package list file, I don't get consistent envs, see #2315 and #2316

@mcg1969 I totally understand what you're saying: "This isn't conda doing something strange".  Broadly speaking, I agree, however there is a pretty rich set of Jupyter extensions and I think we need to understand that Jupyter + Extensions + Anaconda + Conda environments is an operational reality where people may be tempted to dump Conda (and perhaps by association distrust Anaconda) if that combination doesn't play well, as opposed to saying "I guess I can't use this really valuable Jupyter extension, because the extension author hasn't implemented it in a Conda-compatible way".

I feel like it is incumbent upon us to pick up the work we started in December around extension configuration, consider the lay-of-the-land post Jupyter 4.2 (which re-worked extension configuration), and then see if there is anything we can do to make Jupyter extensions have a happier existence with Conda environments.  If that isn't achievable then we can at least consider how extensions could be created/adapted so as to play nicely with Conda environments (while also working in non-Conda environments), then clearly document "Best Practice for Jupyter Extension Configuration to work with Conda" and circulate it in the community.

@GitMensch  

There are many reasons why I would like to keep control on the compiler output. Off the top of my head I can see the following reasons:

  - I need to know the full output path even before running the compiler, for checking modification time and avoid needless compilation
  - I need to be able to specify a build directory (this is especially important for #227)
  - I also need it to implement the clean action

So I think there is no way I will drop the -o option. 

I can determine the right extension by running ``cobc --info`` and by looking at ``COB_MODULE_EXT`` and ``COB_EXEXT`` but I don't like this solution as it is not supported by old compilers and those old compiler are still lurking arround in many popular and stable GNU/Linux distributions (e.g. ubuntu still provides OpenCOBOL 1.1 from many years ago).

So the idea I have in mind at the moment is to simply add two more options to the compiler preferences:
  - executable extension: would default to ".exe" on windows and "" anywhere else
  - module extension: would default to ".dll" on windows, ".so" on GNU/Linux and ".dylib" on OSX

Thoughts?

if you just pass in `"http://artur:m@m@localhost:9200"` it will also work as we parse that correctly. If you pass in the `username:pass@host:port` otherwise we don't try and parse it as an http url since it's lacking the schema.

My assumption was that people would pass in just `host:port` or be explicit by passing in either `{'host': 'localhost', 'http_auth': 'user:pass'}` or the full URI `http://user:pass@localhost:9200/`. This in-between was not expected.

In the tracer the idea is for the code to be reproducible for anyone running elasticsearch, or to be used to paste somewhere public with the description of a technique or a problem. Therefore we put in `localhost:9200` explicitly as to not reveal possibly sensitive information and allow people to paste examples created using the tracer into their command line without need for edits.

1) bgpSettings is a property, not a collection. Thus, `--add` is not the appropriate option to use. 
2) The property names in a JSON string should be snake_cased, not camelCased.

The following command worked:

`az network local-gateway update -g {rg} -n {name} --set bgpSettings="{\"asn\":12312,\"bgp_peering_address\":\"10.1.1.1\", \"peer_weight\":50}"`

After investigation, I found several link that indeed shows uuid1 and thread is a complicated story:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25007264/uuid-uuid1-uuid-generate-time-and-threads
https://github.com/andymccurdy/redis-py/issues/601

Fast workaround, at the beginning of your app:
```python
import uuid
uuid.uuid1 = uuid.uuid4
```
Do this *only* if you are sure your application or any of the not Azure one does not depend on the uuid1 behavior to work.

Secure workaround:
Disable the automatic x-ms-client-request-id header and set it yourself for each call:
```python
# For each client
client.config.generate_client_request_id = False

# For each call, pass this:
custom_headers={'x-ms-client-request-id': str(uuid.uuid4())}
```
If you don’t provide this header, the call will work but you might disable part of the cache system of ARM and slow down your app.

Side note for reference:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1785503/when-should-i-use-uuid-uuid1-vs-uuid-uuid4-in-python

    731 
    732         if self.embed and self.format not in self._ACCEPTABLE_EMBEDDINGS:
--> 733             raise ValueError("Cannot embed the '%s' image format" % (self.format))
    734         self.width = width
    735         self.height = height

ValueError: Cannot embed the 'gif' image format

@Jeffiy — thanks for bringing this excellent point up! The issue is less related to the operating system and more related to some research in UX. People generally read English from left to right, so when you have the action oriented button on the right, the user has more time to think about the decision. If the action button is one the left, you are more likely to hastily click it without bothering to find out what the "non action" button will do. There are a couple of other reasons for this to, that you can read about at [this article](http://uxmovement.com/buttons/why-ok-buttons-in-dialog-boxes-work-best-on-the-right/) if you are curious.

I'm haven't done very thorough testing, but I just spent about ten minutes in a fresh notebook and this is what I'm seeing:

## In code cells

Strange space formatting:

* Will occur immediately after any delimiter
  * e.g. `=`, `,`, `.`, `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}`, `"`, `'`, etc.
* Will not occurs within closed delimiters
* Will not occur within compound statement if closed by `:`
  * e.g. it will occur here `if x =={cursor}`, but not here`if x =={cursor}:`

## In markdown cells

Strange space formatting occurs:

* Unless cursor is at start of a new line
* Unless cursor is placed immediately after closed markup delimiter (e.g. `*text*{cursor}`)
* Unless within markup delimiters (e.g. `*text{cursor}*`)

Hope this helps!

Nathaniel, It would be great if you wanted to reach out to the Berkeley
IRB. Eventuall, we would also have to involve the IRB at Cal Poly as well,
but this would a good start.

The other point that I found with our IRB is that if we were simply
gathering data that we would use *privately* to improve the software, that
would not meet the "generalizable knowledge critieria" and no review was
required. However, when I told them the project/software was open source
and that we should want to share the data/results with the broader
community of the project, they said "that is publication" so IRB approval
and human subject consent would be required.

If we are OK defining a small set of "researchers" that will be able to see
the data and results, but not sharing anything with the public, I think we
have a good amount of freedom. But the second we want to share the
information, I think we have to bring the IRB in. One question is this: can
we limit access to the raw data to a small set of defined "researchers" and
share summaries of the results publicly with out IRB review. Based on my
previous discussions with them, my guess is not.

The other big red flag is that we are talking about creating a general
Python API that any Python code can call to gather information. While we
may not ourselves gather sensitive human subject data, other libraries
could use those APIs to gather than information. If *we* ever deployed the
notebook with central data collection enabled (if we don't, none if this is
useful) we would need to ensure that third party python packages are not
using our API s to send us sensitive data.

Cheers,

Brian

> What do you (@jvivian) and @arkal define "a pipeline" to be?

My operating definition is that a pipeline = a workflow = a stitched together DAG of jobs. Given that definition, I don't think of STAR or BWA or etc as jobs because they're composed of multiple different jobs (e.g., download_sample, download_indices, align_reads, etc).

We have the same definition.  STAR and BWA aren't part of a pipeline until someone does the wiring.  They can be defined as stand alone functions with inputs that are pipeline-independent, with a return statement for the output of STAR that is also pipeline generic. 

> Do you plan to have the aligners in aligners.py implement a common interface? 

I was striving for arguments to the job functions that are pipeline independent. Generally, I've been making the arguments be FileStoreIDs of the inputs and nothing else.  No vague / enigmatic "config" or "inputs" objects/dicts.   E.G.

```
def index_bam(job, bam_id):
    """
    Runs samtools index to create (.bai) files

    :param JobFunctionWrappingJob job: passed automatically by Toil
    :param str bam_id: FileStoreID of the bam file
    :return: BAM index FileStoreID
    :rtype: str
    """
    work_dir = job.fileStore.getLocalTempDir()
    job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(bam_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'sample.bam'))
    # Call: index the bam
    parameters = ['index', '/data/sample.bam']
    docker_call(work_dir=work_dir, parameters=parameters,
                tool='quay.io/ucsc_cgl/samtools:0.1.19--dd5ac549b95eb3e5d166a5e310417ef13651994e')
    return job.fileStore.writeGlobalFile(os.path.join(work_dir, 'sample.bam.bai'))
```

> How do you handle all the tools having different sets of parameters?

Arguments are just the file inputs, and things like cores if necessary.  The one thing I haven't really ironed out is the best way to make the parameters to the tool configurable, but that's not an issue that's being created by this proposal.  I'm thinking since we've moved to YAML configs, we could try storing the parameters into the config, but it's a bit tricky since the parameters are tweaked in every function to reflect input file names, dynamic # of cores, etc. 

An example for MuTect.
```
def run_mutect(job, normal_bam, normal_bai, tumor_bam, tumor_bai, ref, ref_dict, fai, cosmic, dbsnp):
```

For symlinks from /etc/ssl/..., something like this would be nice:

    -rw-r--r-- 1 root root     2.1K Jan 17 17:54 cert1.pem
    -rw-r--r-- 1 root root     1.1K Jan 17 17:54 chain1.pem
    -rw-r--r-- 1 root root     3.2K Jan 17 17:54 fullchain1.pem
    -rw-r----- 1 root ssl-cert 3.2K Jan 17 17:54 privkey1.pem

These settings should be fine for e.g. Debian 8 / Apache.

I guess that users have different requirements (e.g. according to distro), so I think the permissions should be configurable via ``cli.ini`` and maybe via parameters (overwriting ``cli.ini`` settings).

I also want to note that wrong permissions (in the sense of user's requirements) cannot necessarily be healed by a later user interaction: Once the private key is readable after being written to disk with weak permissions, it could theoretically be stolen if the admin user changes permissions too late. Therefore, an _emtpy_ file with correct file permissions must be written to disk _before_ key material is written into the file.

I suggest to introduce three config values for ``cli.ini`` (including example settings):

    private-key-file-owner = root
    private-key-file-group = ssl-cert
    private-key-file-permisson = 640

Optionally, this could also be done for public key material (just for completeness).

I'm not sure for other distros than Debian, if there is a ``ssl-cert`` group (or equivalent). But assumed, such a group is commonly used, this could be a nice default for LE (with unchanged ``cli.ini``).

The first issue is not an issue, just an unfortunate choice (in my opinion) that was made in the original parser implementation. The current behavior is that *all* missing components are filled in from the `default` date, which defaults to today. Off the top of my head, I'd say a better implementation would be if all missing components *larger* than the largest present component were filled in from the default date, and all missing components *smaller* than that would be filled in with the minimum value in that field. That said, that would be a major API change, and I'm not going to do it without a pretty long deprecation cycle with an option to default to the old behavior.

If you know you're parsing ISO8601 datetimes (not including bare ISO-8601 times), you can probably get something equivalent to this with this: `parse(dt_str, default=datetime(datetime.now().year, 1, 1))`, since the only ISO-8601 format without a year is `--MM-DD`.

Regarding the other two:

1. We should probably support `24:00` as an alias for `0:00`. That may be a bit more complicated than it sounds.
2. That's a fairly uncommon ISO-8601 format, in my experience, and I think not a great one. Technically there are fractional time components (e.g. `parse('2010-02-18 16.2h')`), so this may be a bug, but again it may be difficult to handle because I'm not 100% clear on what situations will allow that `16` to be unambiguously detected as an hour.

The dedicated ISO-8601 parser does not have the same choice of default behavior, and it does support `24:00` as an alias for midnight. It will likely eventually support fractional time components as well.

That is why I verify the combination of this data using `@validates_schema`. In this method I build a dictionary (`errors`) with specific field names/error list, expecting that the Unmarshaller builds its own error dict with this input as field-specific error messages instead of schema-level errors.

My use case seem to be solved by instantiating the ValidationError that I raise inside the `@validates_schema`-method with the kwarg `field_names=errors.keys()` so Unmarshaller will not default to `field_names = [SCHEMA]` in combination with a change I propose:

                 else:
                     errors.setdefault(field_name, []).append(text_type(err))

> Schema.load() returns DataTime objects that are timezone aware.

I find this sentence a bit misleading.

A naive input that goes through serialization and back will become aware because it is serialized as aware UTC.

But deserializing a naive datetime will produce a naive output.

```py
def is_naive(d):
    return d.tzinfo is None or d.tzinfo.utcoffset(d) is None

dates = (
    '2014-12-22T03:12:58.019077+00:00',
    '2014-12-22T03:12:58.019077+00:10',
    '2014-12-22T03:12:58.019077Z',
    '2014-12-22T03:12:58.019077',
)

# Using field directly
dates_deserialized = [
    ma.fields.DateTime().deserialize(date)
    for date in dates]

dates_naive = [is_naive(date) for date in dates_deserialized]

assert dates_naive == [False, False, False, True]


# Or equivalently through Schema
class Test(ma.Schema):
    dt_field = ma.fields.DateTime()

dates_deserialized = [
    Test().load({'dt_field': date})[0]['dt_field']
    for date in dates]

dates_naive = [is_naive(date) for date in dates_deserialized]

assert dates_naive == [False, False, False, True]
```

So by itself, `Schema.load()` does not always return `DataTime` objects that are timezone aware. This is true if the datetime was serialized using `Schema.dump()`, but it could come from another source.

Aside from the fact the the `with ... ` block is unnecessary since you don't do anything inside it, it should work fine. 

The file will be created, closed (inside `create()`), then opened (by `connect()`) and closed again (when the `with` block ends).

No. After fitting with the full set, the model uses only the features for

Have you read the LightFM paper? The model works by estimating latent
vectors and bias terms for features; the representation for an item is then
a weighted sum of its feature vectors. The methods I mention give you these
sums given an estimated model and feature matrices. You can then computer
the scores efficiently using the latent vectors (taking their inner
product) and adding item biases.

I fully agree that the polynomial function is a poor choice for the AOI characteristics.  However I would recommend implemting it without clipping at 1.0 for these reasons:

- The original model doesn't have the clipping, so it would not be an accurate implementation of that model.
- If the original model had clipping, the fit coeffiencients would be different, so it would not be strictly correct to use the coefficients from one with the other.

On a related note, I have definitely measured values > 1.0 for some irradiance sensors.  I think it could happen with modules as well, with certain surface textures.  I always thought that values > 1.0 with the SAPM model were just an artifact of the polynomial fit, but am now curious to learn more about the actual measurements.  (Incidentally, there are sensors that respond beyond 90 degrees as well.)

I am of course also in favour of improving various models, but they should be identified as improved models rather than take the place of existing ones, especially if the existing one have been around for a while.

Well, this is a pertinent question.

The arguments you raised seems not legitimate since `utils.POLYMORPHIC_ANCESTORS` checks for modules present in the environment, so if you forgot to install one of the modules, your models are not polymorphic in that sense. This is also a dynamically constructed tuple, so if both modules are available, anybody can use both seamlessly.

The main advantage of the auto-detection approach is to support these polymorphic modules without requiring extra configuration.
But this is definitely less flexible (other polymorphic modules like @adaptivdesign's one are simply ignored) and the whole thing would require extra maintenance and in some way, pollute the code.
So I'm totally open to declare these in a `JSON_API_POLYMORPHIC_ANCESTORS` setting and use this instead. That would require a little paragraph in the documentation.

> You can definitely scan without needing that shape directly - that kind of defeats the point of scan. If you have an implementation online I can take look and use the right variable names, etc. to describe what I mean.

The problem I had in mind was when we have an initial hidden state vector (even if you provide previous hidden states as input whenever possible, at some point you *do* need an initial hidden state because the observed sequence starts and ends somewhere), we need to replicate it `batch_size` times to be able to pass it to scan. In our code, this happens via a dot product here: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/recurrent.py#L357-L358
Just making it broadcastable across the batch dimension didn't work. (At least that's what Søren found.)

> As far as I know, you *have* to compile a function unless you want to track shapes in the .name or .tag (which is what I had before - not pleasant).

Theano has a `getOutputShape()` interface for Ops: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/master/theano/tensor/nnet/conv.py#L254
It's just that it's not required for Ops to implement, that's why you need `theano.function`. If `FAST_COMPILE` does something clever avoiding compilation, it might be useful to tap into that, though.

> Isn't one of the points they make that it's important to also backpropagate through the normalisation operation? Or am I confusing this with another paper?

That would be interesting, but I don't remember reading that. If anybody wants to re-read the paper to check, let us know! I won't experiment with it any time soon.

> Just interesting, what kind of a speed up would it give, in your opinion?

I don't think it would be easy to write a kernel that's a lot faster than what Theano does by default. But not propagating through the normalisation operation would remove most of the backpropagation costs. It's a bit more difficult to implement (you'd need to wrap the operation in an Op and override the gradient definition, this could be done via [`OpFromGraph`](http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/library/compile/opfromgraph.html) and setting its `grad()` member to a new function). And we don't know if that would defeat the purpose, you'd need to check if Sander's memory of the paper is better than mine in this respect.

By the time of the last release, VFG was undergoing some re-writing (you should be able to see that in the commit history). The proper solution includes enabling/implementing a few features, not only in VFG itself.

- Enable the max-iteration support. Set a upper limit of traversal iterations for each basic block. There is a global variable for that matter (at the beginning of vfg.py, the `MAX_ANALYSIS_TIMES` variable), but as far as I remember, in the current release, that variable is not used.
- Implement widening support. The existing code for widening in vfg.py is intentionally disabled.
- Implement fix-point detection. The released version does not do a fix-point detection since there was some flaws in SimuVEX's state merging implementation. Some of us were actively working on a better state merging a couple of days ago.

We are making some major refactoring and bug-fixing on angr, as well as other underlying libraries. A quick look at my angr repo shows that we are almost 200 commits ahead of the current GitHub master. If you'd like to fix the code or implementation, my honest opinion is to wait until the week of Aug. 4th - that's when we will release all our changes. Those VFG issues will be fixed, and you'll have a working and useable VFG :-)

Feel free to ask any question regarding the current version.

```python
import xmltodict
import base64
import hashlib
from Crypto.Cipher import AES
def parse_refund_result(xml):
    BS = AES.block_size
    pad = lambda s: s + (BS - len(s) % BS) * chr(BS - len(s) % BS)
    unpad = lambda s: s[0:-ord(s[-1])]

    data = xmltodict.parse(xml)['xml']
    req_info = data['req_info'].encode()
    req_info = base64.decodebytes(req_info)
    hash = hashlib.md5()
    hash.update(current_app.config['WECHAT_PAY_API_KEY'].encode())
    key = hash.hexdigest().encode()

    cipher = AES.new(key, AES.MODE_ECB)
    decrypt_bytes = cipher.decrypt(req_info)
    decrypt_str = decrypt_bytes.decode()
    decrypt_str = unpad(decrypt_str)

    info = xmltodict.parse(decrypt_str)['root']
    data = dict(data, **info)
    data.pop('req_info')
    return data
```

I think the functionality has been in mpv for a while.

Current filtering cases that need to be handled consistently:

1. Filter filenames based on filename metadata including start and end time
2. Filter file handlers based on internal file (or data) start and end time (file handler properties)
3. Filter file handlers based on geographic area
4. Filter file handlers based on file metadata (`fh.metadata` dictionary)

So 2, 3, and 4 all require opening the files first. I believe @mraspaud and @pnuu and I discussed this on Slack, but to implement 2, 3, and 4 requires the user to do:

```
scn = Scene(..., reader_kwargs={'metadata': {'start_time': datetime(...)}})
```

Or similar. Is that too ugly? Because of how flexible this could be I don't think it makes sense to use `**kwargs` collection to the `Scene` and have that pass it along to the file handler filtering.

Another option for 2, 3, and 4 is to separate out the file handler filtering to a separate `Scene` method like `filter_data_sources` or something similar. The file handler filtering already has to create the complete file handler object so this isn't wasting processing time as far as that is considered.

Lastly, besides filtering by area, start time, and end time, is there really other metadata that someone would filter by that couldn't be taken from the filename? I suppose there is always that chance. Plus this does allow for some really fancy filtering later on. For example if the file handler wanted to do a "quality control" check on `__init__` the user could then filter out all bad files `scn.filter_data_sources(quality_threshold=0.8)`.

Thoughts @pnuu @mraspaud @adybbroe?

@rajanandakumar the code which deals with the amount of available threads and queue-ing of tasks is the same for both the user and monitoring threads.

Are you seeing monitoring commands which have been queued and not run when there are many available monitoring threads?

I'm assuming that you're using the DIRAC backend? in which case the monitoring system is supposed to queue additional commands to be run on the monitoring threads which are exposed through the 'queues' command. These commands are constructed by the monitoring loop as it finds a job which needs updating.

If there are commands which have been queued and not running then there is a bug in the Monitoring queues system.

If there are simply not many commands being queued at once then this could be due to a bottleneck in the monitoring loop code. (This ganga job monitoring code is sometimes extremely slow when first starting up and when dealing with large numbers of (sub)jobs. As I've said I'm working on optimising this.)


I hope this clears things up a bit,

Thanks,

Rob

To expand on the process of securing the API key when you _are_ building a production app:

The most common way to do this is, as you mentioned, having the client code access an intermediate server to get information from the API while the server makes the actual requests to the API. This "hides" the API key behind the server and removes the need for the client code to know it.

**Cassiopeia is not currently designed for use in client code under this architecture**. I've heard from some users that they're using the type system Cass defines in client code without using the API request component, but there's currently no way to use Cass "in the normal way" in client code under this architecture. As a note, **this is one feature we're hoping to add in an upcoming™️ major release**.

That out of the way, it's probably worth mentioning that outside of games written entirely in python, it seems to be fairly rare that the client code for applications like this are written in python. Since most projects surrounding the Riot API tend to be websites, it's far more common for python to be used as the backend & server, with HTML/CSS/JS being used on the frontend & client code. If you're looking to go a similar route, we _do_ have an alpha-stage project to help bootstrap this type of setup. [kernel](https://github.com/meraki-analytics/kernel) is an extensible API proxy server, which will hide your key as well as handling (redis) caching & rate limiting for you. If you'd like to try using it, I'm happy to help you set it up & get working with it. Keep in mind, though, that some breaking changes are likely in that project's future.

Best,
Rob

It's entirely possible for me to stub out the signing code when running the tests, though I'd rather keep the service as it is in prod. 

My qualm is that the contracts aren't being verified based on what exactly is sent, but rather by how the json is encoded: 
```
json.loads('{"a": "b"}') == json.loads('{"a":"b"}')
'{"a": "b"}') != '{"a":"b"}'
```

@adamcharnock, after taking a deeper look at the gist here are some thoughts:
* Why `convert_form_field_to_djangomodel` returns an ID? (If I'm querying for a model inside a model, aka `user.group.name`, I want to get the whole instance for the group, not just the id)
* What you think about having `SimpleQuerySetConnectionResolver`,`FilterConnectionResolver` as function decorators? So Someone can actually do something like
```python
class Query(ObjectType):
    # ...
   @filter_connection
    def resolve_all_accounts(self, args, info):
        return Account.objects.filter(user=info.context.request_context.user) # Only for my user
```
* We could also create a Filter class dynamically (aka `django_filters.FilterSet`) when using `DjangoFilterConnectionField`, similar as [Django Rest Framework is doing](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/filtering/#specifying-filter-fields), something like:
```python
class Query(ObjectType):
    account = relay.NodeField(accounts_nodes.Account)
    all_accounts = DjangoFilterConnectionField(accounts_nodes.Account, fields=['name', 'slug', 'created'])
```

@nibanks & @LuDuda & @qiangg  - The goal of this PR is to add a very simple 'debug uart' for logging (and other) purposes to the embedded platforms.   Your target is code is/was effected in some non-trivial way and I would like your comments and/or review.

The two major items are: 

**Item 1 of 2** - In the process I stumbled across a **BUG** I found is here:

https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/radio.h#L110

This data structure changes size based upon a CONFIGURATION option

The bug is this: Various platforms include "config.h" type files in different order, some BEFORE radio.h, and some AFTER radio.h, some do this via a series nested includes, it is a tangled web of includes.  If the RAW LINK is enabled, some target code ends up with a size(X) structure, and other code has size(Y) - not a pretty thing.

FYI - @qiangg the emsk (ARC core) target is where I found this problem, the feature that causes this problem was added well after the ARC core was introduced (so it is a new bug not something that has existed). I just happened to stumble across this bug via compiler warnings and errors using Travis, that said - it can easily effect multiple targets that do the same thing - please review my changes to your target

@jwhui  - Not sure how you want this fixed long term, I'm just the messenger here, I think it would be better to have stable data structures in all cases.

The solution I arrived at is, `#include <openthread/config.h>` and `#include <openthread-core-config.h>` at the top of various files...

That caused a cascade of problems.... in two non-TI targets - hence I would like some other review.

- @nibanks - (windows target) I had to modify the command line parameters for several projects adding the `-DOPENTHREAD_CONFIG_FILE="openthread-windows-config.h"` in several places - please review.

- @LuDuda  - (nordic target) there are/where quite a few repetitive changes in the Nordic example. I'm sure you are aware of, and have already seen the problems with the GNU autoconf macro "PACKAGE" and the member variable in your BSP data structures. I believe I have correctly mitigated the changes, I've added some comments explaining the purpose of the `#undef PACKAGE` - however this is not my code, and I'm not sure how you would like this represented and/or explained. Your input would be helpful here.

**Item 2 of 2 - new otCliLogLEVEL()' macros** 

I came across an interesting problem in the CLI code, all when trying to understand a timing related bug, it is described here in detail in the "cli_uart.cpp" changes (look for the log macro invocation)

To solve this, I add a new set of LOG macros for the CLI module (they did not exist before) 

@nibanks - two things (A) I had to make some changes to: `openthread/platform/logging-windows.h` 
 and I have no idea if I did this correctly, or not - please take note of, and review these changes.  I know it compiles :+1:  but don't know if it is correct.

@nibanks - Second item(B) I believe the "multi-instance" feature is substantially a Windows feature. In the cli_uart.cpp code, in order to call otPlatLog(), we must pass an instance pointer to the log macro, I don't seem to understand how to get/extract the instance pointer when multi-instance is enabled. More importantly, I think any change like that to this need to be tested, and I don't have a build system for Windows and domain knowledge in how to debug windows drivers.  Thus: for now, logging at that point is disabled in the multi-instance case, you might want to look into this at some later time.

def do_flip(dec=None, inc=None, di_block=None):
    """
    This function returns the antipode (i.e. it flips) of directions.

    The function can take dec and inc as seperate lists if they are of equal
    length and explicitly specified or are the first two arguments. It will then
    return a list of flipped decs and a list of flipped incs. If a di_block (a
    nested list of [dec,inc,1.0]) is specified then it is used and the function
    returns a di_block with the flipped directions.

    Parameters
    ----------
    dec: list of declinations
    inc: list of inclinations

    or

    di_block: a nested list of [dec,inc,1.0]

    A di_block can be provided instead of dec, inc lists in which case it will
    be used. Either dec, inc lists or a di_block need to passed to the function.
    """

Per offline discussion with @lukesneeringer and @bjwatson, the current state of things will be maintained. In summary:

- The package name that appears on PyPI (e.g., `proto-google-cloud-errorreporting-v1beta1` and `gapic-google-cloud-errorreporting-v1beta1`) will be consistent and derived only from the artman config.
- The package *path* within these PyPI packages (e.g., `google.cloud.proto.devtools.clouderrorreporting`, `google.cloud.gapic.errorreporting`) may be inconsistent because the Python-language proto package is derived from the proto-language proto package, and the GAPIC package is derived from the GAPIC config.

I found the problem:
On TF <= 1.1.0 `recurrent.py` imports `static_bidirectional_rnn` which works.
On TF >= 1.2.0 `recurrent.py` imports `bidirectional_dynamic_rnn` which is not compatible with the current implementation.

Removing the input for `bidirectional_dynamic_rnn` solves it. PR inbound.

Hi @jgc128,

The `-pretrainedVectors` option was added mostly to initialize the word embeddings of supervised models with vectors which were trained in an unsupervised fashion. Do you want to initialize an unsupervised model (`cbow` or `skipgram`) with pre-trained vectors?

If so, I believe that using the `-pretrainedVectors` option is the proper way of doing this (I have never tried this though). It will initialize the embeddings and then fine-tune these on the target corpus.

By the way, you can also try to use our recently released word vectors, trained on Wikipedia and common crawl, which are available on the fastText website: https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/english-vectors.html.

As of now, it is not possible to load pre-trained binary models.

Please feel free to re-open this task if this does not answer your questions!

Best,
Edouard.

> The common practice today seems to be to have dedicated nodes within the network that serve as parameter servers, 

That is common practice, and is what distributed Tensorflow uses.

> accepting and aggregating updates from workers, and periodically publishing those back out to workers.

In the vanilla case, we only want to update `parameters` from the `parameters` we sent to other workers.

In the most simple use case for distributed tensorflow, each worker passes around some parameter `global_step` that identifies when `parameters` was pulled from the parameter server, and gathers updates from a certain number of workers. The update is discarded if the `global_step` a worker sends back is stale. A good reference for this is "[Scaling Distributed Machine Learning with the Parameter Server][2]".

[2]:https://www.usenix.org/system/files/conference/osdi14/osdi14-paper-li_mu.pdf

This pub/sub framework LGTM. Is there any way to have a worker subscribe to a topic published by another worker in particular? Something like `Sub('foo', worker=1)` or `Pub('foo', worker='nearest_neighbor')`. This could enable some of the distributed algorithms (e.g., where nodes are connected to their neighbors and form a ring).

Two extensions:

* Async read/writes could be useful, and enable some new algorithms (e.g., [Hogwild!][1]). 
    * Async read/writes := having some mechanism so two workers can write to parameters at the same time, and read partially written results. This is probably best accomplished by using threads.
* Gradient coding, which codes the gradient ndarray to some object (typically a dictionary). 
     * Two function are defined: `encode: ndarray -> dict` and `decode: dict -> ndarray` so `decode(encode(x)) == x` (well, or maybe only approximately). This would involve the worker running `updates_pub.put(encode(result)`, and the master running `update(parameters, decode(new_data))`.

I'm inclined to believe these extensions are best suited for a class interface, at least privately.

[1]:http://papers.nips.cc/paper/4390-hogwild-a-lock-free-approach-to-parallelizing-stochastic-gradient-descent.pdf

Hey Mathias,

If you want to run a script at time of application invocation, you can directly pass arguments such as a load command on the command line like so:
```bash
python job.py @test.cmd
```
Or equivalently:
```bash
python job.py "load test.cmd"
```

In either case you don't need to manually feed the cmdqueue so the bottom of your script can just be:
```python
if __name__ == '__main__':
    job = Job()
    job.cmdloop()
```

If you want to do something more complicated or exotic, you can manage your own event loop instead of running the cmd2.Cmd.cmdloop().  For an example of how to do this see the **event_loops.py** example in the *examples* directory.  In essence you can add whatever timed control flow you want and you call each individual command with ``onecmd_plus_hooks`` and you make sure to call ``preloop`` at the beginning and ``postloop`` at the end.

@wjakob On a semi-related note, the stable version of the docs is outdated: http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/changelog.html. Updating the stable branch to v2.1 requires a force push so I wanted to check in, rather than just do it. As an alternative, RTD considers the newest tagged release to be stable so the branch isn't strictly needed and can be simply removed -- RTD would automatically update when new tags are pushed.

In this specific case it would probably be a good idea to use `pybind11/stl_bind.h`'s `bind_vector` ([see the docs](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/stl.html#binding-stl-containers)).  It provides a python-like wrapper (e.g. `append` rather than `push_back`) around a C++ vector type, with other nice goodies like slicing support.

(It looks like an omission that we don't have a `clear` method, which Python 3.3+ lists *do* have.  You can add additional definitions to the `class_<...>` object returned by `bind_vector`, of course, but adding `clear` would be an easy PR, if you're up for it).

As to the actual issue, one possible solution is avoid binding stl functions directly, but instead use thin lambda functions that wrap the stl functions rather than directly binding the stl functions.  e.g. `.def("clear", [](std::vector<double> &v) { v.clear(); })`.

The only disadvantage of the current implementation, as I see it, is that you can't define methods / properties on the newly created enum class, because it's not a `class_<>`. In fact, `py3_enum` is not even an `object` (should it be, somehow?).

Hypothetically, here's also an alternative implementation: define py3 enums through `class_`, somewhat similar to the current `enum_`. This, however, will require two features of `class_` that we don't currently have:
- Accept a class dict to be used at the type instantiation site -- because many metaclasses (like those of enum types) use that. This could be also a useful thing to have in general. I tried it out in a local branch and it's a trivial addition. However, it's not very useful without having the next point implemented.
- A non-trivial part is actually calling metaclass's `__new__()` if it's provided, similar to what `builtins.__build__class__()` does -- we currently don't, so metaclass support is somewhat half-baked and is limited to post-class-creation use.

Also, in this case you won't be able to do `enum_<>().value().value().def()` anyway since the full dict of entries is needed at type instantiation point, so it would either have to be `enum_<>().value().value().finalize().def()`, or `enum_<>(...=..., ...=...).def()`.

With the creation of the more specific issue #4830, I think this issue can be closed. To respond to each of the original points:

> Users should be able to control several aspects of this:
>
> * Do they get ciphersuites optimised for compatibility, for security, a tradeoff betewen those, or in accordance with a specific project's recommendations (Mozilla/Bulletproof TLS/etc)

As suggested in the original post, we went with a tradeoff using [Mozilla's intermediate configuration](https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/Server_Side_TLS#Intermediate_compatibility_.28default.29).

I think the majority of the Certbot team disagrees that users should be able to control this through the Certbot UI though. Choosing things like ciphersuites is a complicated decision that most users probably shouldn't be making. Additionally as a more minor point, it adds extra complexity to Certbot with little benefit to most users.

Instead, Certbot provides a sane default configuration which users can tweak as the desire outside of Certbot. The way this interacts with automatically the configuration is explained below.

> * Do they get other security features optimised for compatibility, for security, or a tradeoff?

This is the purpose of #4830.

> * Are changes to these things automatically applied after a client update, or only at manual sys admin request?

Since Certbot 0.15.0, the way this works in both the Apache and Nginx plugin is the SSL/TLS configuration is placed in Certbot's configuration directory (`/etc/letsencrypt` by default). When Certbot is updated, Certbot checks whether this file has been modified by the user. If it has, Certbot explains that it cannot safely automatically update your configuration and prints/logs the location of the new file which the user can use as a reference when manually updating their configuration. If it has not been modified by the user, Certbot updates the file. This allows experienced sysadmins to tweak settings to their hearts content while still providing security updates to the majority of Certbot's users.

With that said, I'm closing this issue. If anyone strongly disagrees, please explain why and I'll consider reopening.

We could potentially move the Dockerfiles to another directory with their own README, but then we'd have to do strange things like pulling Certbot with `git` rather than using the local copy which seems bad.

[ci integration] added. As for the timeouts, as far as I understood we prefer to let the curl/http calls time-out. I agree that we're agnostic towards onchain events to actually happen at some point, but we can also handle failed transactions. Having timeouts inside the state machine wouldn't make sense, as the blocktime varies widely and is outside of our scope. Additionally, we would have to define states for timeouts, which again, wouln't really make sense w.r.t. to blocktime variation.

To check what is going on,

1. Check if you are using python 3.6
```
$ python --version
Python 3.6.2 :: Anaconda custom (64-bit)
```

2. Check if julia is in the output of `jupyter kernelspec list`

```
$ jupyter kernelspec list
Available kernels:
  ruby         /Users/bpeng1/.ipython/kernels/ruby
  bash         /Users/bpeng1/Library/Jupyter/kernels/bash
  ir           /Users/bpeng1/Library/Jupyter/kernels/ir
  julia-0.6    /Users/bpeng1/Library/Jupyter/kernels/julia-0.6
```

3. Check if you can use julia-0.6 kernel separately (`New` -> `Julia 0.6.1`).

4. Check if sos julia language module is installed

```
pip install sos-julia
```
The following command should not produce any error
```
python -c 'import sos_julia.kernel'
```

5. Create a SoS notebook, check if `Julia` (capital `J`) is listed as one of the languages. If you see `julia 0.6.1`, then the language module is not working.

6. Then you should be able to use `%use Julia` or select `Julia` from language dropdown box.

The first thing I notice is that sos execute tasks in this order

1. generate all tasks 
2. create all task files, combine them in groups if `trunk_size` is specified.
3. sending all of them to execute, and wait for all of them to be completed
4. The master process accepts all tasks and start sending them to workers

Obviously, if there are many tasks, each step can take a while and the tasks should better be run async...

@robertnishihara, that test fails because of this new commit: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/commit/ff2217251f99e325b36f7ee6ee59d17e0f2d4cd4.

Here's what happened:
1. in main thread, `local_scheduler_client.get_task` holds the lock and blocks until the a new task is received.
2. in import thread, here in the `push_erorr_to_driver` function (https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/aa423318442c18e3e73b6125a5c5237258d60ff0/python/ray/worker.py#L1976), the local_scheduler_client is also used to push_error, but it can never get the lock in this case.

I think, even without my PR, there's still a bug in the current code. Because local_scheduler_client is used in 2 threads, there might be a race condition when 2 threads tries to use the underlying connection at the same time.

Potential solutions:
1) import thread uses its own local_scheduler_client
2) make local_scheduler_client thread-safe (in c++ code)
3) kill import thread, fetch functions on demand

What do you think?

This is the guide that I've used before. https://gist.github.com/domenic/ec8b0fc8ab45f39403dd#get-encrypted-credentials. These are the steps to get a new set of keys:

1. Generate a new ssh key pair with no passphrase.
2. Install the `travis` program (it's a ruby gem)
3. Encrypt the private key with the `travis` program
4. Modify the call to the `openssl` command to use the new env variables in `script/pushdocs.sh` 
5. Add the public key to the `streamlink.github.io` repo, remove the old one.
6. Replace `deploy-key.enc` with the new encrypted private key file
7. Push the changes to the `streamlink` repo.
8. Delete the ssh key pair from your computer.

If you don't know how git or github works and if you don't want to go this route, you can also simply use https://gist.github.com/, which is a code snippet tool used for linking larger code files or multiple files together.

And although pictures usually say more than thousand a words, images containing pure text should be posted as text alone.

Great, that's what kibitzr is for.
First, you'll need to update your version to at least 2.5.1 (just released) to have SMTP support.
Then copy these lines to kibitzr.yml:
```
pages:

  - name: JetPack updates
    url: https://wordpress.org/plugins/jetpack/changelog/
    transform:
        - css: "#plugin-info > div.block-content"
        - text
        - changes
    notify:
        - smtp:
            recipients:
              - <you email here@gmail.com>
            subject: JetPack update
    period: 3600
```
And change "<you email here@gmail.com>" with an e-mail where you want to receive updates  (TO: field). You can provide several e-mails here
Then create kibitzr-creds.yml with contents:
```
smtp:
    host: smtp.gmail.com
    port: 587
    user: <you email here@gmail.com>
    password: <you email password here@gmail.com>
```
Make sure to change user and password. This user is for FROM: field.

I suggest creating a new conda environment and reinstalling as above with parallel-ssh *from pip*. If you can replicate then make a spec list file from that environment (`conda list --explicit > spec-file.txt`) and please paste it here to reproduce that environment. 

Please also test with `ParallelSSHClient(<..>, timeout=120)` to ensure server timeout is not truncating output.

It's a bit tricky, in that we made the tiling design of widgets and the size of the label for the slider before we had a selection range slider. We don't know how big the item text will be, so we can't easily make it always work and still have widgets tile nicely. So we have a default width for the slider, and make it easy to make it bigger if the things you are selecting have wider text.

So I would say it's a limitation of our current design with an accepted workaround, more than a bug.

I found this from the `ipv6(7)` manpage: > The default value for [`IPV6_V6ONLY`] is defined by the contents of the file /proc/sys/net/ipv6/bindv6only. The default value for that file is 0 (false). I did a bit of testing, and found this behavior with respect to the sockets created by `accept`:
connecting listening accept
socket type address type socket type address type socket type v6only value
AF_INET6 ipv6 AF_INET6 ipv6 AF_INET6 1
AF_INET6 mapped-ipv4 AF_INET6 mapped-ipv4 AF_INET6 0
AF_INET6 mapped-ipv4 AF_INET ipv4 AF_INET6 0
AF_INET ipv4 AF_INET6 mapped-ipv4 AF_INET X
It seems like because: * accept-v6onlyness seems correct to begin with * we should probably respect the system default anyway * `IPV6_V6ONLY` doesn't seem to be supported on all INET6 sockets ... that we should remove [trio/socket.py#L191](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/master/trio/socket.py#L191)
I guess `IPv4 encapsulation` doesn't exist on windows? Maybe we replace this with something just as ancient as TCP, like UDP or ICMP?

Here's an example script using cspline:

```
from gekko import gekko 
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

"""
minimize y
s.t.     y = f(x)

using cubic spline with random sampling of data
"""

# function to generate data for cspline
def f(x):
    return 3*np.sin(x) - (x-3) 

x_data = np.random.rand(50)*10+10 #generate random data points
y_data = f(x_data)

c = gekko()
x = c.Var(value=np.random.rand(1)*10+10) #initialize at random point
y = c.Var()
c.cspline(x,y,x_data,y_data,True)
c.Obj(y)
c.options.IMODE = 3
c.options.CSV_READ = 0
c.solve()

plt.figure()
plt.scatter(x_data,y_data,5,'b')
plt.scatter(x.value,y.value,200,'r','x')
```

SORRY!

> 第一，目前pyecharts对于地图管理还没有形成有效和规范的方法，不宜此时向外提供接口，这无疑扩大了问题领域。

0.3.2是最佳时机，因为0.3.2是成立 pyecharts organisation 的第一个包。与0.3.1相比，所有的内部架构全部更新了。与其一步一步挪，0.3.2一步到位的话，省去我们中间为每一步出现问题的解答时间 和 给中间版本支持的时间。比如 为了支持 0.3.1， 我需要另成立 一个 [jupyter-echarts read-only](https://github.com/chfw/jupyter-echarts) 项目 以保持 0.3.1的 jupyter notebook 用户的承诺。试想，我们中间的改动, 从 pyecharts-cli 到 lml 的改动，如果发布出去，我们可要维护 pyecharts-cli 了。

> 第二，推广方面，个人倾向于“捆绑式”推广pyecharts，以pyecharts这个名称为中心，包括代码仓库、网站等。

新立的 echarts-maps 是把所有的地图 js 项目放在一块，这样不会把 pyecharts organisation 的项目弄太多，覆盖了 python 的项目。我预期近期会有这些 js 包： echarts-united-kingdom-js, echarts-china-counties-js （中国县级地图）, echarts-china-custom-js(华北，华南，钓鱼岛等),echarts-us-js, echarts-australia-js, echarts-india-js。 再远一点的将来，我预期会有这些包： echarts-china-history-map-js (秦汉唐宋元明清等) 。

> china 其实没有必要分的这么详细，可以全部放在一起。

这里的考量是文件包的大小。每个包的大小不一样，echarts-china-cities-pypkg 有 3.8MB, echarts-china-provinces-pypkg 是 730 K。需要省图的，就不需要市图了。将来会再增加 echarts-china-counties-js, 这个县级地图包估计会是 3.8MB以上。

> 这里面要考虑的问题很多，比行政区划变更、行政区划级别、名称重名、多音字、中文名称规范，没有一个很好的规划，就暂时不要更改了，还是用原来的

中文拼音是有以上问题，不过它们相对于用户来说，他们是不需要知道的。换句话说，就是这些拼音可以用 uuid 代替。为了方便我们维护，所以用了pinyin。等我们需要面对非中文开发人员的时候，这个部分还将要改动，因为他们不会打中文。

'post' parameter is not a part of the original paper on hnsw. It is an nmslib-only feature.
Yes, having no post parameter decreases the accuracy a bit at high recalls (up to 10-15% speed), but it allows index updates, much less build memory and much faster indexing time. Because of the latter two reasons it is off by default in nmslib.

hnswlib is likely to have an updates friendly substitute for post parameter in the future.

By default ef is 20 in nmslib. I would recommend always tuning ef for an accuracy/speed tradeoff that is optimal for your application.

This behavior is correct.  Python ints are equal to java longs in Python3 and thus need to be made into java Long when placed on a generic object.  The behavior is currently different for Python2 where both int and long exist.  

In general you should specify the type of object when converting a python primitive to a java object by using the appropriate wrapper type.

```
arrayList.add(jpype.JInt(123))
```

Please try to see this from the point of a user.
You can't make the old API vanish, neither from the code nor the minds of the people.

See, on IRC there are still questions on where the device programs are.
They _disappeared_ long ago, but the memory is still alive.

The only way to get a migration going is to provide the alternative and _explain_ what the benefits are.
